Capital Investment Decisions and Project Evaluation at Target Corporation
Growth Estimates, Sustainable Growth Rate (SGR), Financial Metrics of Mature Companies, and Capital Allocation Decisions
Performance Measurement, Capital Budgeting Practices, and CFO Decision-Making (Survey of CFO Practices vs. Finance Theory)
Cancelled session — professor absent, no lecture delivered
Replacement Decisions and Adjusted Present Value (APV)
Replacement decision analysis in a declining industry (Aurora Textile Company); investment horizon and salvage value effects on NPV
Valuing IT Investments in Mature Companies; Non-Traditional Metrics for Technology Projects; IT Investment Evaluation Processes
Project Valuation and Operating Leverage (Fonderia di Torino Case)
Lease vs. Buy Decisions; Accounting Treatment of Operating vs. Finance Leases; NPV Analysis of Leasing vs. Owning Equipment

Foreign currency hedging: forward contracts, futures, options, financial vs. operational hedging, and operational hedging strategies (risk shifting, leading/lagging, real options from plant locations)
Modeling investments in a multinational setting: exchange rate risk, repatriation risk, foreign-currency DCF/APV, and hedging strategies
Corporate diversification: measurement, value creation vs. destruction, diversification discount/premium, related vs. unrelated diversification, global vs. domestic diversification

Capital structure theories (pecking order vs. trade-off), lifecycle effects on leverage, Microsoft case study, cost of equity with leverage, financial distress costs, financing source comparison
Capital structure optimization: debt vs. equity trade-offs, share repurchases, and recapitalization analysis using Hill Country Snack Foods case
Capital structure for multinational companies; Country risk premiums and cost of equity for foreign subsidiaries
Dividend Policy: Theories of dividend preference, clientele effects, information asymmetry, tax considerations, and firm lifecycle effects on payout policy
Stock Buybacks vs Dividends; Minerva Financial Crisis Discussion
Financial Restructuring - Debt Restructuring Mechanisms, Asset Restructuring
Operational Restructuring and Cost-Saving
Dividend Policy Case Study (Rokoboro/Rockborough)

concepts.name: Company lifecycle stages
concepts.definition: A classification framework dividing a company's life into stages: startup, growth, expansion, established, mature, decline. Used to think about what financial decisions are appropriate at each stage.
concepts.context: Students chose companies and identified their lifecycle stage as pre-classwork. Professor clarified that the stages are a stylized model - companies don't have to follow them linearly or go through every stage.

concepts.name: Dominant objective vs. binding constraint
concepts.definition: When analyzing a financial decision, identify the primary goal (dominant objective) and the main limiting factor (binding constraint) that shapes the decision.
concepts.context: Students were asked to identify these for their chosen companies' financial decisions during breakout groups. E.g., AstroTalk's dominant objective was setting a valuation benchmark via pre-IPO round, with equity dilution as a constraint.

concepts.name: Pecking order of financing
concepts.definition: A hierarchy of financing preferences: (1) internal financing / self-funding from profits, (2) debt funding, (3) equity financing / dilution. Companies prefer internal funds first, then debt, then equity as a last resort.
concepts.context: Discussed during breakout about AstroTalk's pre-IPO funding decision. The company's internal funds (250 CR / ~$30M) were insufficient for expansion, debt would add pressure on a barely profitable company, so equity round was chosen.

concepts.name: Evolving role of the CFO
concepts.definition: The CFO role is shifting from traditional accounting/financial management to strategic leadership. Technology automates routine accounting, allowing CFOs to focus on management performance, value creation, M&A execution, and compliance. Business unit experience is valuable for informed strategic decision-making.
concepts.context: Opening poll/discussion based on McKinsey reading. Students debated whether CFOs need operational/business unit experience or primarily strategic capability.

concepts.name: Capital allocation in maturity: reinvest vs. distribute
concepts.definition: Mature companies face the choice of reinvesting profits into capex/growth opportunities vs. distributing cash to investors via dividends or buybacks.
concepts.context: Discussed with Meta's decision to freeze dividends and invest retained earnings into AI capex. Professor framed it as a classic maturity-stage dilemma.

concepts.name: Stress testing financial decisions
concepts.definition: Evaluating how a financial decision would change under different scenarios: capital market access tightening, cost of capital rising, regulatory changes, competitive shifts, government intervention.
concepts.context: Each breakout group was asked to apply a stress-test prompt to their company's financial decision. Professor emphasized that good decisions can have bad outcomes due to uncertainty.

concepts.name: Public benefit corporation structure
concepts.definition: A corporate structure that requires pursuing both profit and social/environmental benefit. May protect companies from shareholder lawsuits demanding profit maximization, but potentially allows companies to avoid returning value to investors.
concepts.context: Discussed regarding OpenAI's conversion to a PBC. Dexter noted OpenAI, xAI, and Anthropic all adopted this structure. Professor highlighted the trade-off: long-term public benefit focus vs. potential to misuse investor capital.

concepts.name: Equity vs. debt financing trade-offs
concepts.definition: Equity financing avoids fixed repayment obligations (good for cash-flow-negative or risky companies), while debt financing preserves ownership but requires interest payments regardless of performance.
concepts.context: Discussed with SpaceX's preference for PE over debt. Many projects fail, so equity's lack of repayment obligation limits downside risk. If capital markets tighten, equity holders cannot demand repayment, unlike debt holders.

concepts.name: Bridge financing / pre-IPO round
concepts.definition: A funding round just before IPO that serves dual purposes: provides capital for the interim period and sets a valuation benchmark that retail investors use to evaluate the IPO price.
concepts.context: Discussed for AstroTalk. The pre-IPO round set a valuation benchmark (5x previous round) and provided capital for risky expansion moves.

concepts.name: Institutional voids vs. macroeconomic/policy factors
concepts.definition: Institutional voids are weaknesses in infrastructure (weak banks, lack of credit, non-independent institutions) that can potentially be filled by entrepreneurs. Currency instability is more of a macroeconomic/policy outcome, not an institutional void per se, though the distinction depends on whether you can do something about it.
concepts.context: David asked whether Buenos Aires currency instability is an institutional void. Multiple students (Anne, Fernando/Tanat, Ece, Bo-Qian) and the professor debated this, concluding it's more policy-driven. Bo-Qian cited Brexit as an example of currency instability from events, not institutions.

concepts.name: Connecting DCF to strategic frameworks
concepts.definition: Porter's Five Forces and other B112 frameworks affect DCF assumptions: buyer power lowers pricing/margins, supplier power raises COGS, competition increases SG&A and lowers gross margins, political instability affects risk-free rate and cost of debt.
concepts.context: Breakout activity connecting B111/B112 concepts to B165 finance. Students mapped five forces to specific DCF line items.

concepts.name: Lifecycle stages are a model, not a rule
concepts.definition: The seven lifecycle stages are an analytical tool, not mandatory steps. Companies can skip stages, revisit them, or not fit neatly into any single stage.
concepts.context: Sonia asked whether companies must go through all seven stages. Dexter said the stages are defined by analysts, not operators. Professor confirmed it's a thinking tool, not a requirement, citing AstroTalk as an example of skipping the established stage.

concepts.name: NPV (Net Present Value)
concepts.definition: A core financial metric used to evaluate whether a project generates returns above the cost of capital; positive NPV means the project creates value for shareholders.
concepts.context: Used throughout the session to compare and rank five Target store investment projects. Professor emphasized NPV as the headline metric but stressed looking beyond it.

concepts.name: IRR (Internal Rate of Return)
concepts.definition: The discount rate at which a project's NPV equals zero; used as a hurdle-rate comparison tool alongside NPV.
concepts.context: Each Target project had an IRR calculated. The Barn had the highest IRR (16%). Goldie Square's IRR of 8.1% was below the 9% WACC, highlighting the project's weakness on retail sales alone.

concepts.name: WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital)
concepts.definition: The minimum required return for the company, representing the blended cost of debt and equity financing. Target's WACC was cited as 9%.
concepts.context: Used as the benchmark discount rate. Goldie Square's total IRR of 8.1% was below the 9% WACC, but credit card revenues at a 4% discount rate pulled the blended NPV positive.

concepts.name: Capital Constraints and Capital Rationing
concepts.definition: When a firm has a limited budget for capital expenditures and cannot invest in all positive-NPV projects, requiring prioritization by value created per dollar invested.
concepts.context: Target had $300M total budget with $200M committed, leaving $100M (or hypothetically $120M) for the remaining projects. Professor showed that under constraints, NPV-per-dollar matters more than absolute NPV.

concepts.name: NPV per Dollar Invested (Profitability Index logic)
concepts.definition: When capital is constrained, the ratio of NPV to investment cost determines which combination of projects maximizes total value creation.
concepts.context: Professor illustrated that under a $120M constraint, investing in The Barn + Gopher Place yields $36M+$37M NPV vs. Whelan Court alone at $25-26M NPV, despite Whelan Court deploying more capital.

concepts.name: Cannibalization
concepts.definition: Revenue loss from existing stores when a new store in the same area draws customers away from those existing locations.
concepts.context: Major discussion topic. Gopher Place had 19% projected cannibalization from five nearby stores. The Barn had zero cannibalization (nearest stores 80-90 miles away). Extended debate on whether cannibalization should be assumed to disappear by year 5 as projected.

concepts.name: Sensitivity Analysis / Sales Volatility Buffer
concepts.definition: Testing how much key assumptions (especially sales) can deteriorate before a project fails to meet its required return.
concepts.context: The Barn could sustain an 18% sales decrease and still achieve prototype NPV. Gopher Place could only handle about 5% decrease. This buffer was used to argue for The Barn as the safest project, especially given the approaching financial crisis.

concepts.name: Opportunity Cost
concepts.definition: The value of the next-best alternative forgone when making an investment decision.
concepts.context: Professor and student Tanmay emphasized that these are not the only projects Target will ever have; capital deployed now cannot be used for future opportunities.

concepts.name: Brand Visibility / Intangible Strategic Value
concepts.definition: Non-financial, hard-to-quantify benefits of investments such as increased brand awareness, market presence, and long-term revenue enhancement from visibility.
concepts.context: Central theme of the session. Students debated how to incorporate soft benefits like urban visibility and brand growth into DCF-based project evaluation. Whelan Court and Goldie Square were projects where brand visibility was a key consideration.

concepts.name: Risk-Adjusted Return
concepts.definition: Evaluating project returns by accounting for the uncertainty and variability of projected cash flows, not just headline NPV/IRR.
concepts.context: Professor emphasized that all projections are estimates and real-world outcomes often deviate. Sensitivity analysis and sales buffers are tools for thinking about risk-adjusted returns.

concepts.name: Differential Discount Rates for Different Cash Flow Streams
concepts.definition: Applying different discount rates to cash flows with different risk profiles within the same project.
concepts.context: Goldie Square used a 4% discount rate for credit card revenues (predictable, low-risk) vs. the 9% WACC for retail revenues. This made a money-losing retail operation appear as a positive-NPV project overall.

concepts.name: Expanded Role of the CFO
concepts.definition: The modern CFO goes beyond bookkeeping and financial statements to become a strategic partner, balancing multiple stakeholders and considering factors beyond pure financial metrics.
concepts.context: Opening discussion topic. Students noted the CFO must balance Wall Street expectations, capital project limits, cannibalization effects, expert opinions, and strategic considerations like brand visibility.

concepts.name: Signaling Value of Large Investments
concepts.definition: Making a bold, visible investment can signal to the stock market that the company is making strategic moves, potentially reinvigorating investor interest.
concepts.context: Ricardo argued that Whelan Court, despite weak risk-adjusted returns, could provide a 'shock' to reinstate Target's stock as attractive, given the stock had been flattening.

concepts.name: Capital Expenditure Committee (CEC) Governance
concepts.definition: A centralized committee of top executives (CIT, CMO, CHRO, CFO, CEO) that reviews and approves major capital investment projects.
concepts.context: Extensive discussion of pros (diverse expertise, rigorous evaluation) and cons (slow process taking 1-2 years, bias toward quantifiable metrics, politics/gaming of assumptions, conflict of interest from stock holdings).

concepts.name: Incentive Compatibility / Agency Problems in Capital Allocation
concepts.definition: The risk that individuals involved in proposing or evaluating projects have personal incentives that conflict with maximizing firm value.
concepts.context: Professor probed whether real estate managers championing projects have compensation tied to project acceptance. Students discussed gaming of assumptions, emotional attachment after 1-2 years of development, and commission-based conflicts.

concepts.name: Competitive Pre-emption
concepts.definition: Investing in a location or market to prevent a competitor from gaining a foothold there.
concepts.context: Gopher Place was argued as strategically important because Walmart was planning to build a super center in that area. Investing preemptively would 'eat the market before they come.'

concepts.name: Prototype Benchmarking (P04)
concepts.definition: Comparing proposed projects against a standardized prototype store model to assess relative performance.
concepts.context: Target's dashboards compared each project's metrics to a P04 prototype. Students debated whether this added useful benchmarking context or noise, since demographics varied widely across projects.

concepts.name: Real Options Thinking
concepts.definition: Viewing an investment as creating the option for future expansion or strategic moves, beyond just the immediate project value.
concepts.context: Ricardo argued Whelan Court had 'unrealized, uncalculable upside potential' and could create real options for expanding in a high-acquisition-power area if successful.

concepts.name: Growth Rate Estimation
concepts.definition: The process of forecasting future company growth, which is inherently difficult and prone to systematic overestimation. Wall Street analysts tend to overestimate growth rates by approximately 100%, driven by incentive structures and reliance on historical data.
concepts.context: Opening discussion based on pre-class reading about mechanisms of estimating future company growth rates.

concepts.name: Analyst Bias / Optimism Bias
concepts.definition: Systematic tendency of analysts to overestimate growth rates due to multiple factors: (1) extrapolating historical high-growth rates forward without accounting for maturity, (2) inability to predict black swan events, (3) asymmetric incentives where underestimating damages relationships with company management, and (4) positive feedback loop between management optimism and analyst optimism.
concepts.context: Discussion of why growth forecasts are systematically biased upward, connected to analyst incentive structures and access to management.

concepts.name: Black Swan Events
concepts.definition: Low-probability, high-severity events (referencing Nassim Taleb) that are sector-defining or market-defining and cannot be foreseen. Examples include COVID-19 pandemic. These events can define markets for the next 10 years.
concepts.context: Raised by a student (Cool) when discussing why growth predictions fail, emphasizing entropy and randomness in markets.

concepts.name: Sustainable Growth Rate (SGR)
concepts.definition: The rate at which a company can grow while keeping its capital structure (debt-to-equity ratio) constant, holding DuPont ratios (profitability, leverage, payout) fixed. Common misconception: SGR does NOT assume no new debt; it assumes new debt in the right proportion to maintain the debt ratio. SGR is a financing identity, not a market or strategy forecast.
concepts.context: Ondra corrected a widespread misunderstanding about SGR that appears even in assigned readings. Professor confirmed SGR requires borrowing proportionally to maintain leverage ratio.

concepts.name: SGR Misconception (No New Debt)
concepts.definition: A systematic misunderstanding where analysts and textbooks claim SGR is the maximum growth rate without borrowing more. In reality, SGR maintains constant leverage, so if equity grows through retained earnings, debt must also grow proportionally. This misconception leads to overly optimistic growth forecasts.
concepts.context: Ondra identified this error exists even in course readings and Beyond55 materials. Professor confirmed the correction.

concepts.name: Act One vs Act Two Growth (McKinsey Framework)
concepts.definition: Act One: Growth by focusing on core product, maximizing monetization, pricing, customer appeal, and management utilization. Act Two: Expanding into new markets, creating new channels, adjacent products, and new realms of growth once the primary product market is saturated.
concepts.context: Based on the McKinsey pre-class reading, applied to Microsoft case study.

concepts.name: Asset Turnover Decline in Maturing Companies
concepts.definition: When sales growth is slower than asset growth, asset turnover (Sales/Assets) declines. This happens when companies retain earnings and grow assets but cannot proportionally grow sales due to market saturation. Borrowing more would make it worse since you can't borrow your way into increasing sales.
concepts.context: Pre-class spreadsheet model analysis comparing Scenario 1 (fixed capital structure, 25% sales growth) vs Scenario 2 (no new debt, only 5% sales growth).

concepts.name: Assets vs Sales Controllability
concepts.definition: Assets and liabilities are largely under management's control (can borrow more, retain more earnings), but sales are exogenous -- dependent on market conditions, demand, competition, and market saturation. You cannot 'magic up' new sales just by increasing assets.
concepts.context: Professor's key insight when explaining why Scenario 2 diverges: asset growth is controllable but sales growth depends on the marketplace.

concepts.name: Long-Run SGR Convergence
concepts.definition: In a scenario where a company retains all earnings but sales grow at only 5%, the SGR will asymptotically approach 5% (the sales growth rate) over time, not zero. Once SGR reaches the sales growth rate, assets and sales grow in tandem and ratios stabilize.
concepts.context: Pre-class model discussion about what happens to SGR in Scenario 2 over time. Students initially guessed zero; professor corrected to 5%.

concepts.name: Dividend Policy and Cash Return
concepts.definition: When a company runs out of positive NPV projects, it should return cash to shareholders (dividends or buybacks) rather than accumulating idle cash. If the company cannot produce returns above cost of capital, investors are better served receiving cash back. Retention ratio can go negative when dividends exceed current net income (drawing on accumulated cash).
concepts.context: Discussion of what Scenario 2 company should do: distribute dividends instead of 100% retention, or take on debt to maintain capital structure.

concepts.name: Negative Retention Ratio
concepts.definition: Occurs when dividends paid exceed net income for the period, meaning the company is returning not just current earnings but also accumulated cash reserves. Not sustainable indefinitely but possible when mature companies with large cash reserves begin aggressive shareholder returns.
concepts.context: Student (Really) asked about negative retention ratios observed in industry average data. Multiple explanations explored including negative net income artifacts and excess cash distribution.

concepts.name: Regular Dividend vs Special Dividend vs Share Buyback
concepts.definition: Regular dividends set ongoing expectations for continued payment. Special dividends carry no expectation of repetition. Share buybacks also have no inherent expectation of continuity, though some companies (like Apple) have ongoing programs. All are mechanisms for returning cash to shareholders.
concepts.context: Dexter asked whether paying dividends once sets expectations. Professor explained the different signaling mechanisms.

concepts.name: Positive NPV Project Threshold for Retention
concepts.definition: Companies should retain earnings only as long as they have positive NPV projects offering returns above the cost of capital. Once those opportunities are exhausted, excess cash should be returned to shareholders. If ROE falls below investors' required return, capital should be returned.
concepts.context: Professor explained the theoretical basis for dividend policy: retain when you have projects earning above cost of capital, distribute when you don't.

concepts.name: Law of Large Numbers (Growth Context)
concepts.definition: As companies become very large (billions in revenue), maintaining high percentage growth rates becomes increasingly difficult. A company at $100M can grow 60%, but at billions, growth typically slows to mature rates (~5%). Applied to Apple, Microsoft, Google whose actual sales growth consistently lags their SGR.
concepts.context: Referenced from readings; discussed when analyzing industry data showing SGR consistently exceeding actual sales growth for large mature companies.

concepts.name: Cash Drag / Idle Cash Problem
concepts.definition: When companies accumulate excess cash without productive use, it earns only risk-free rates (Treasury), destroying shareholder value. Activist investors, hedge funds, or private equity may force cash distribution. Some companies invest idle cash in mutual funds or bonds to show profitability, which is not operational efficiency.
concepts.context: Tanmay observed startups showing profitability from mutual fund investments rather than operations. Professor connected to broader theme of returning excess cash.

concepts.name: DuPont Analysis Components in SGR
concepts.definition: SGR is derived from ROE components (profit margin, asset turnover, leverage) multiplied by retention ratio. In Scenario 2, all DuPont components decline: asset turnover falls (sales lag assets), leverage falls (no new debt), leading to declining ROE and SGR.
concepts.context: Breakout room and full-class discussion of Scenario 2 dynamics in the pre-class spreadsheet model.

concepts.name: Industry Maturity and Retention Patterns
concepts.definition: Mature industries (building materials, paper, broadcasting) tend to have lower retention ratios and return more cash. Growth industries (software, tech) retain more earnings seeking value-creating opportunities. The appropriate retention policy depends on industry life cycle stage.
concepts.context: Industry average data analysis showing patterns across broadcasting, building materials, paper/forest products, entertainment, software, semiconductors.

concepts.name: Net Present Value (NPV)
concepts.definition: A dollar-denominated measure showing the difference between the amount invested and the present value of returns. Positive NPV means the project creates value; negative means more is invested than produced.
concepts.context: Reviewed at the start of class as foundational B111 knowledge before discussing how CFOs actually use capital budgeting tools.

concepts.name: Internal Rate of Return (IRR)
concepts.definition: A percentage-based return metric that should exceed WACC to indicate value creation. The spread between IRR and WACC represents value created (e.g., 15% IRR vs 10% WACC = 5% value creation). Must always be used as a point of comparison.
concepts.context: Reviewed alongside NPV as foundational capital budgeting metrics from B111.

concepts.name: Adjusted Present Value (APV)
concepts.definition: A valuation technique that separates the value of a project into two components: (1) the unlevered project value (discounted using unlevered cost of capital, as if financed entirely with equity), and (2) the financing effects (tax shields from debt, minus issuance costs). Instead of incorporating debt into WACC, debt effects are valued as a separate step.
concepts.context: Ricardo brought it up from the readings; professor confirmed it will be covered in detail in a future session. Noted as procedurally simpler when debt levels fluctuate.

concepts.name: Payback Period
concepts.definition: A capital budgeting metric measuring how quickly an investment returns its initial cost. Does not inherently account for time value of money. Favored especially by smaller, capital-constrained firms with higher uncertainty.
concepts.context: Discussed extensively when analyzing the CFO survey results. Students debated whether payback period considers time value of money. Professor emphasized its value in capital-constrained and high-uncertainty contexts.

concepts.name: Earnings Management
concepts.definition: The practice of companies (both public and private) prioritizing meeting earnings targets, sometimes at the expense of long-term value creation. Involves smoothing earnings for credibility and predictability.
concepts.context: Central topic of the prep poll discussion. Students explored why even private companies care about earnings as much as public ones.

concepts.name: Financial Flexibility
concepts.definition: Prioritizing the ability to access capital (maintaining good credit ratings and borrowing capacity) over strictly minimizing WACC or hitting specific leverage targets. CFOs in the survey ranked this above optimizing capital structure.
concepts.context: Emerged from group discussion of the survey findings. Riri noted it was surprising that flexibility trumped the theoretical optimal capital structure.

concepts.name: Trade-Off Theory vs. Pecking Order Theory
concepts.definition: Trade-off theory: firms optimize capital structure by balancing tax benefits of debt against costs of financial distress. Pecking order theory: firms prefer internal funds first, then debt, then equity due to information asymmetry costs. Survey showed real-world behavior diverges from pure trade-off theory, especially for smaller firms.
concepts.context: Ural pointed out that smaller firms follow pecking order more because information costs are higher for them, while larger firms can afford to think about trade-off optimization.

concepts.name: Convertible Debt
concepts.definition: A financing instrument offering lower interest rates (due to built-in conversion option) that converts to equity if share price rises. Acts as a compromise between debt and equity -- only issues equity at favorable prices.
concepts.context: Professor noted convertibles were viewed favorably in the survey and have continued to increase in popularity since the early 2000s as a way to sidestep the debt vs. equity preference dilemma.

concepts.name: Company-Wide vs. Risk-Adjusted Discount Rates
concepts.definition: Using a single company-wide discount rate for all projects (as ~60% of surveyed CFOs did) rather than project-specific risk-adjusted rates. This penalizes less risky divisions and overinvests in riskier ones.
concepts.context: Andra raised this as inconsistent with finance theory. Professor agreed it leads to suboptimal investment decisions but noted it may have improved in the 20+ years since the survey.

concepts.name: Managerial Overconfidence / Optimism Bias
concepts.definition: The tendency of CFOs to believe their company is undervalued by the market. The survey showed nearly universal belief among CFOs that the market underestimates their company's growth potential.
concepts.context: David highlighted this from the survey. Professor compared it to '90% of people think they're above-average drivers.' Ondra raised the possibility of selection/participation bias in the survey (only 9% response rate).

concepts.name: Market Timing of Equity Issuance
concepts.definition: CFOs issue equity when they perceive stock price is high (overvalued) and repurchase shares when they perceive stock is low (undervalued). This is not insider trading because it benefits existing shareholders collectively.
concepts.context: Dexter asked whether this constitutes insider trading. Professor explained the distinction: insider trading harms shareholders for individual gain, while corporate repurchase/issuance decisions benefit shareholders collectively.

concepts.name: Insider Trading vs. Corporate Actions
concepts.definition: Insider trading is when individuals use non-public information to profit personally at shareholders' expense. Corporate decisions to issue or repurchase shares based on management's view of valuation is legal because it benefits shareholders as a whole.
concepts.context: Dexter asked about the distinction; professor gave a detailed explanation of the SEC logic -- the key distinction is whether shareholders are harmed or benefited.

concepts.name: Incentive Design and Feedback Loops
concepts.definition: How compensation structures (bonuses tied to stock price or EPS targets) can distort decision-making. Earnings targets create reinforcing feedback loops that can inflate expectations and encourage underinvestment in R&D/marketing.
concepts.context: Sonya introduced system dynamics perspective; Carl discussed unrealistic expectations from Wall Street. Professor emphasized that stock-price-based compensation ties rewards to things employees cannot control.

concepts.name: Future Earnings Guidance vs. Quarterly Results
concepts.definition: The market reacts more strongly to future earnings guidance than to whether a specific quarter's earnings target was met. If management provides credible forward-looking guidance explaining an earnings miss, the market incorporates that information within about a month.
concepts.context: Professor's own insight during the incentive design discussion, arguing that obsessing over quarterly earnings is somewhat misguided if you have a credible long-term story.

concepts.name: Selection Bias in Survey Research
concepts.definition: The possibility that survey results are skewed because only certain types of respondents (e.g., more optimistic CFOs) chose to participate, especially with a low 9% response rate.
concepts.context: Ondra raised this as a caveat to the CFO survey findings, particularly regarding the overconfidence result.

concepts.name: System Dynamics in Financial Decision-Making
concepts.definition: Analyzing how incentives create reinforcing feedback loops and time-delayed effects. Earnings targets can create self-reinforcing cycles of inflated expectations, similar to overestimated synergies discussed in session 3.
concepts.context: Sonya introduced this concept via chat due to audio issues. Professor asked Arush to champion the idea, then Sonya got audio working and explained how incentives create reinforcing loops.

concepts.name: EPS as Internal vs. External Metric
concepts.definition: NPV/IRR used internally for project evaluation (pursuit of true economic value), while earnings/EPS used externally for communication with analysts, markets, and boards (comparability and constraint on managers).
concepts.context: Final reflection poll response. A student synthesized the two activities, noting they serve different audiences and purposes rather than contradicting each other.

concepts.name: Adjusted Present Value (APV)
concepts.definition: A valuation approach that separates the value of a project into three components: (1) value of the project as if all-equity financed (unlevered), (2) the present value of tax shields from debt, and (3) the expected costs of financial distress. Contrasted with WACC approach which bundles everything into one discount rate.
concepts.context: Core topic of the session; introduced via student summary at the start, then explored through a detailed numerical scenario with perpetuity cash flows.

concepts.name: Replacement Decision Valuation
concepts.definition: Investment analysis for replacing existing equipment/processes rather than investing in new growth. Key principle: must evaluate incremental cash flows (difference between replacement scenario and status quo), not total cash flows from the new asset.
concepts.context: Opened the session discussion; students explained why mature firms prioritize replacement over growth investments. Also the subject of the pre-classwork spreadsheet exercise.

concepts.name: Incremental Cash Flows
concepts.definition: The difference in cash flows between two scenarios (with replacement vs. without replacement). Only the incremental differences matter for project evaluation, not absolute amounts.
concepts.context: Undraal explained this as comparing two separate realities; professor emphasized it as the key consideration in replacement decisions.

concepts.name: Tax Shield from Debt
concepts.definition: The tax savings generated because interest payments are tax-deductible. Calculated as: Tax Rate x Debt x Interest Rate (annual savings), then discounted as perpetuity at the cost of debt. In perpetuity form: Tax Rate x Debt (since interest rate in numerator and denominator cancel out).
concepts.context: Central to the APV scenario discussion. Professor walked through the math: $10M debt at 5% = $500K interest, at 40% tax rate = $300K annual tax savings, discounted at 5% = $6M (but example showed $4M with different parameters).

concepts.name: Gordon Growth Model (Perpetuity as Special Case)
concepts.definition: The perpetuity valuation (PV = CF / r) is a special case of the Gordon Growth Model with zero growth rate. Used in the APV example to value $1.2M annual cash flow at 10% discount rate = $12M.
concepts.context: Carl identified the all-equity valuation in Scenario 1 as a Gordon model application; professor confirmed and praised the connection.

concepts.name: Hamada's Equation (Relevering Beta)
concepts.definition: The relationship between unlevered beta (project risk without debt) and levered beta (equity risk with debt). Used to calculate the required return on equity after adding debt by first finding unlevered beta from comparable companies, then relevering for the target capital structure.
concepts.context: Ece recalled from B111 how to calculate the new required return on equity when leverage is introduced; professor confirmed and named Hamada's equation.

concepts.name: Financial Leverage and Equity Risk
concepts.definition: When a company borrows debt, the remaining cash flows to equity become riskier because debt holders must be paid first (fixed obligation). Any percentage change in underlying business results in a larger percentage change in equity cash flows. Cost of equity rises with leverage.
concepts.context: Ada raised this point; professor elaborated on the interplay between higher cost of equity and potentially lower after-tax cost of debt.

concepts.name: Costs of Financial Distress
concepts.definition: Both direct costs (higher interest rates on additional borrowing) and indirect costs (loss of B2B contracts, customer reluctance to buy long-term products from a potentially failing company). Missing from the simple APV scenario but required for complete APV analysis per Damodaran.
concepts.context: Andrel identified this as the missing component from the APV example; professor gave examples of indirect costs like B2B contract difficulties and customer hesitancy.

concepts.name: APV vs. WACC Comparison
concepts.definition: Both should yield the same result under identical assumptions. APV advantage: breaks down value into operational vs. financing components, and allows for changing capital structure over time. WACC assumes constant capital structure throughout the project, making it harder to adjust for changes in debt ratios.
concepts.context: Ann asked about sensitivity differences; professor explained they are equally sensitive but APV provides better decomposition of value sources and flexibility for varying capital structures.

concepts.name: MACRS Depreciation
concepts.definition: Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System - a government-prescribed depreciation schedule that allows faster depreciation than straight-line. Different asset classes have statutory percentages. Percentages are applied to original book value (not declining balance) and sum to 100%. Companies prefer MACRS over straight-line because earlier depreciation yields earlier tax shields.
concepts.context: Discussed during pre-classwork review; students were confused about percentage-based depreciation applied to original cost rather than declining book value. Professor clarified it is a legal requirement by asset class.

concepts.name: Tax-Adjusted Sale of Old Equipment
concepts.definition: When selling equipment below book value, the loss is tax-deductible. The tax-adjusted sale value = market value + tax savings from recognized loss. Example: selling at $70K with book value $120K = $50K loss, tax savings = $50K x tax rate, so total = $70K + tax savings = $87K.
concepts.context: Professor walked through why selling old equipment at $70K yields $87K in tax-adjusted value in the replacement decision spreadsheet.

concepts.name: Economies of Scale in Replacement Decisions
concepts.definition: For mature, large companies, even a 1% operational improvement translates to material dollar amounts, making replacement investments worthwhile. Small companies would not benefit enough to justify the effort.
concepts.context: Sonia made this point; professor confirmed it as a key reason mature firms prioritize replacement over growth investments.

concepts.name: Perpetual Debt and Rollover Assumption
concepts.definition: When valuing a perpetuity project financed with debt, assume the debt is also perpetual (rolled over when it matures). Even if issuing a 10-year bond, assume re-issuance at similar terms to simplify the perpetuity framework.
concepts.context: Undraal questioned why perpetuity formula applies to debt that is not actually perpetual; professor explained the rollover assumption.

concepts.name: Break-Even Price with Downstream Effects
concepts.definition: The maximum price for a new asset is NOT simply current cost + NPV, because a higher purchase price increases the depreciable base, which increases tax shields, which further increases NPV. Must iterate (e.g., Goal Seek) until NPV = 0.
concepts.context: Final discussion point; Undraal suggested adding NPV to cost, but professor demonstrated that this is non-linear because higher cost creates more depreciation tax shields.

concepts.name: Cognitive Biases in Investment Decisions
concepts.definition: Overconfidence bias (overestimating cost savings from upgrades), sunk cost fallacy (reluctance to replace existing 'perfectly good' equipment), and over-optimism about operational improvements.
concepts.context: Professor noted these biases as caveats to replacement decisions even though they have lower uncertainty than growth investments.

concepts.name: Credit Evaluation Multi-Factor Model
concepts.definition: Banks and credit rating agencies evaluate creditworthiness using multiple factors: cash flow volatility, interest coverage ratio (EBIT/interest), debt-to-equity ratio, and other metrics to determine lending limits and rates.
concepts.context: Dexter asked how banks decide credit limits; professor explained it is analogous to how rating agencies assign bond ratings (BB vs. AA).

concepts.name: Working Capital Changes in Replacement Projects
concepts.definition: Changes in inventories and accounts payable from replacement decisions create one-time working capital effects at project start (and reversal at end). Timing is discretionary (year 0 or year 1) since changes happen gradually over the year.
concepts.context: Major source of confusion in pre-classwork; breakout room discussion and professor review addressed conflicting instructions about whether working capital changes existed.

concepts.name: Replacement Decision Analysis
concepts.definition: Evaluating whether to replace existing equipment (old machine) with new equipment (Zinsser spinning machine) by comparing the NPV and IRR of two separate cash flow scenarios versus using incremental cash flows directly.
concepts.context: Core analysis of the Aurora Textile case: should the company replace old machinery with a new Zinsser spinning machine that produces higher quality yarn at greater efficiency but costs $8.25 million upfront.

concepts.name: Separate Scenario Modeling vs. Incremental Cash Flows
concepts.definition: Two approaches to evaluating replacement decisions: (1) model full cash flows for status quo and new machine separately, then compare; (2) calculate incremental cash flows directly. Both should yield the same result, but separate scenario modeling provides more transparency, is less error-prone, and easier for stakeholders to review.
concepts.context: Pre-class poll discussion about the pros and cons of each modeling approach. Professor concluded that separate scenario modeling is generally preferable for important decisions, while incremental is useful as a quick thinking tool.

concepts.name: Hurdle Rate
concepts.definition: The minimum rate of return a project must achieve for management to approve the investment. Often equals the WACC unless the project has different risk characteristics, in which case it can be adjusted upward (higher risk) or downward (lower risk).
concepts.context: David asked what the 10% hurdle rate means and how it relates to IRR of 29%. Anne explained it is management's required return threshold, often equal to WACC.

concepts.name: Investment Horizon Risk
concepts.definition: The risk that a company may not survive long enough to realize the projected future cash flows from an investment. If the company goes bankrupt before the payback period, the investment destroys value.
concepts.context: Jakub raised the point that management was confident about 10-year returns but uncertain about the company's survival. The second half of class modeled how NPV changes when the assumed survival horizon is shortened.

concepts.name: Option Value of Cash / Real Options
concepts.definition: The strategic flexibility that having cash on hand provides. By committing all capital to one large investment, a company gives up the ability to respond to future opportunities or threats.
concepts.context: Ece argued Aurora could wait until the old machine is fully depreciated before investing. Professor framed this as giving up option value by locking capital into one machine now.

concepts.name: Salvage Value and After-Tax Salvage
concepts.definition: When equipment is sold or scrapped before the end of its useful life, the company receives the sale price plus a tax benefit on any capital loss (book value minus sale price). After-tax salvage = sale price + tax rate * (book value - sale price). Even with zero sale price, the company benefits from the tax deduction on the capital loss.
concepts.context: Breakout activity in the second half of class modeled NPV under different survival horizons. The distinction between ignoring salvage value entirely and claiming a zero-price salvage (which still yields tax benefits) was a key teaching point.

concepts.name: Working Capital and Inventory Changes
concepts.definition: The change in net working capital driven by inventory policy: new machine companies keep 20 days sales in inventory vs. 30 days for old machine. This creates an upfront cash inflow (inventory reduction) but raises questions about modeling accuracy.
concepts.context: Anne and Karl questioned why inventory changed in 2002 before the new machine was installed, and why the modeled inventory levels were inconsistent with the historical balance sheet. The purchasing manager said inventory decisions are based on cotton prices, not production method.

concepts.name: Inventory Recapture at Project End
concepts.definition: Whether to assume inventory is sold off and cash is recovered at the end of a project's analysis period. Recapture applies for finite-lifetime projects; for going concerns (company continues operating), inventory is not recaptured.
concepts.context: Anne asked about recapturing inventory at the end of the 10-year horizon. Professor explained that since the company will keep operating, they should not assume inventory is liquidated.

concepts.name: Training Expense Tax Treatment
concepts.definition: Training costs for a new machine are fully expensed immediately (not capitalized), so the after-tax training cost = training expense * (1 - tax rate). This contrasts with capital expenditures, which are depreciated over time.
concepts.context: Yiran asked why training is listed as after-tax while other costs are not. Professor explained training is expensed immediately and receives an immediate tax write-off, unlike CAPEX which is depreciated over the machine's useful life.

concepts.name: Sensitivity Analysis
concepts.definition: Testing how changes in key assumptions (growth in sales, price changes, defect rates, survival horizon) affect project NPV and IRR. Critical when margins are thin and assumptions are uncertain.
concepts.context: Arush recommended sensitivity analysis given thin margins and uncertain assumptions about revenue growth and returns rates. Professor agreed this was particularly important for Aurora.

concepts.name: Credit Rating and Financing Constraints
concepts.definition: A BB (below investment grade / 'junk') credit rating makes it difficult and expensive to raise additional capital, constraining the company's ability to finance large investments.
concepts.context: Bo-Chen and Yiran noted Aurora's BB rating means the company would struggle to borrow the $6 million needed (machine costs $8M, company has only $2M cash). This increases distress costs and limits strategic flexibility.

concepts.name: Distress Costs
concepts.definition: Indirect costs that arise when stakeholders (suppliers, customers) perceive that a company lacks liquidity or is at risk of bankruptcy, potentially leading to worse terms and reduced business.
concepts.context: Ricardo noted that depleting liquidity for this investment could increase distress costs, as suppliers and partners would know the company has no other options.

concepts.name: Asymmetric Liability for Defects
concepts.definition: In the textile industry, when a customer returns a defective product, the raw materials supplier must reimburse far more than the original cost of materials, creating a disproportionately large liability.
concepts.context: Dania explained this as an industry feature affecting Aurora: returns to JCPenney required full product reimbursement, not just the yarn cost, making defect rates a critical financial risk.

concepts.name: NPV Function Mechanics in Excel
concepts.definition: The NPV function in Excel automatically discounts all cash flows within its range. Cash flows outside the function (e.g., time-zero investment) must be added separately. Additional items like salvage values at specific future dates must be manually discounted if added outside the NPV range.
concepts.context: Professor demonstrated proper NPV formula construction in cell D13 during the investment horizon exercise. Jed asked a follow-up question about when manual discounting is needed vs. when the function handles it.

concepts.name: Non-Traditional Metrics for IT Investment Evaluation
concepts.definition: Using metrics beyond standard DCF/NPV to evaluate technology investments, including qualitative KPIs, mission alignment, and custom company-specific metrics
concepts.context: Opened the session as the main takeaway from pre-class readings; companies like Philips were cited as examples of developing custom evaluation metrics

concepts.name: Intangible Benefits of IT Investments
concepts.definition: Benefits that are difficult to quantify financially, such as improved brand reputation, smoother internal processes, employee effectiveness, and strategic flexibility
concepts.context: Discussed as a core challenge when evaluating IT investments; NPV misses 'soft parts' like reputation, brand value, and process improvements

concepts.name: Real Options Thinking for IT Investments
concepts.definition: Treating IT investments as options that provide future flexibility rather than all-or-nothing commitments; includes options to delay, stage, or expand investments based on pilot results
concepts.context: Arush raised the comparison of NPV to real options from the readings; professor confirmed this helps quantify soft parameters and supports staged/pilot investment approaches

concepts.name: NPV Analysis for IT Projects
concepts.definition: Standard net present value analysis applied to IT investments, including modeling cash flows from cost savings, revenue impacts, and working capital effects
concepts.context: Students modeled two scenarios for a software system update in pre-classwork; discussed limitations when applied to projects with intangible benefits

concepts.name: Opportunity Cost in Non-Profit IT Decisions
concepts.definition: The value of alternative uses of funds when a non-profit invests in IT rather than directly funding its mission (e.g., scholarships, emergency funds)
concepts.context: Jakub identified opportunity cost as the key concept when discussing whether DCF is useful for non-profits evaluating IT investments

concepts.name: Price Elasticity of Demand as Key Assumption
concepts.definition: The assumption that reducing product price will lead to increased unit sales; critical for estimating benefits in IT investment scenario 2
concepts.context: In scenario 2, the assumption that a $1 price decrease would increase sales by 1,500 units (5%) was identified as one of the most important and uncertain assumptions

concepts.name: WACC as Discount Rate for Internal Projects
concepts.definition: Using the company's weighted average cost of capital as the discount rate for internal IT investment evaluation, appropriate when the project has similar risk to the company's overall operations
concepts.context: Discussed whether 10% WACC was appropriate for the IT investment scenarios; students debated whether IT projects carry different risk profiles than core operations

concepts.name: Sensitivity Analysis on Discount Rate
concepts.definition: Testing how sensitive NPV results are to changes in the discount rate, especially when the project's true risk is uncertain
concepts.context: Professor suggested sensitivity analysis as a response to uncertainty about appropriate discount rates for internal IT projects

concepts.name: Working Capital Effects of IT Investments
concepts.definition: How IT systems can affect accounts receivable (faster collection), accounts payable (early payment discounts via automation), inventory (better forecasting/management), and cash (reduced idle cash through better forecasting)
concepts.context: Detailed class discussion on each component of net working capital and how IT investments can improve each; Carl gave comprehensive initial answer covering AR, AP, and inventory

concepts.name: Cash as Non-Productive Asset
concepts.definition: Idle cash sitting on the balance sheet is generally unproductive; better IT systems can reduce required cash cushions by improving forecasting and operational turnaround times
concepts.context: Professor emphasized this when discussing how IT investments affect net working capital beyond just AR, AP, and inventory

concepts.name: IT Investment Approval Process Design
concepts.definition: A formal organizational process for evaluating, approving, and auditing IT expenditures, including dollar thresholds for different approval levels, business case requirements, and post-implementation audits
concepts.context: Second half of class focused on a role-play exercise evaluating a hypothetical IT investment approval methodology with thresholds at $5,000, $10,000, and $50,000

concepts.name: Post-Audit of IT Investments
concepts.definition: Reviewing completed IT projects to compare actual results against initial estimates, serving as benchmarking and accuracy-checking rather than punishment
concepts.context: Bo Chen raised concern about whether post-audit would become a punishment system or genuinely drive improvement; Ricardo explained it helps benchmark teams' estimation accuracy

concepts.name: Bureaucracy vs. Innovation Trade-off
concepts.definition: The tension between having robust approval processes to prevent waste/gaming and maintaining enough organizational agility for employees to innovate and act on time-sensitive opportunities
concepts.context: David and Ondra raised concerns that too much process kills innovation; professor agreed and noted the importance of company culture in determining appropriate process levels

concepts.name: Risk Management in IT Investments
concepts.definition: Considering cybersecurity, data privacy, legal compliance, and confidentiality risks beyond what a discount rate adjustment can capture
concepts.context: Chen and Ada raised that the proposed evaluation process lacked risk assessment; professor noted some risks cannot be quantified simply by adjusting the discount rate

concepts.name: Scenario Comparison (Status Quo vs. Incremental)
concepts.definition: The choice of baseline when comparing investment scenarios matters: comparing both scenarios to status quo vs. comparing scenario 2 incrementally to scenario 1 yields different NPV results but same ranking
concepts.context: Jakub raised that scenario 2 should use scenario 1's reduced IT costs as baseline; professor clarified both approaches are valid but the solution compared each to status quo

concepts.name: AI Investment Valuation Challenge
concepts.definition: The difficulty of measuring and estimating the value and ROI of AI implementations, as a current real-world parallel to the general IT investment valuation problem
concepts.context: Tanatswa connected the readings to the current wave of AI investment, noting the difficulty of measuring value from AI and converting it to numbers

concepts.name: Operating Leverage
concepts.definition: The ratio of fixed costs to variable costs in a company's cost structure. Higher fixed costs relative to variable costs means higher operating leverage. Can be measured as contribution margin divided by EBIT.
concepts.context: Introduced at the start of class as the foundational concept for the Fonderia di Torino case, where the company considers replacing labor-intensive production (variable costs) with an automated machine (fixed costs).

concepts.name: Relationship Between Operating Leverage and Financial Leverage
concepts.definition: Both operating leverage and financial leverage involve fixed costs (fixed operating costs vs. fixed interest payments) that amplify the volatility of returns to equity. When things go well, leverage magnifies gains; when things go poorly, leverage magnifies losses.
concepts.context: Professor drew the parallel to explain why operating leverage is called 'leverage' - same mechanism as financial leverage but applied to the cost structure of operations rather than the capital structure.

concepts.name: Fixed vs. Variable Cost Trade-off
concepts.definition: The strategic decision between investing heavily in fixed assets (machines, buildings) that reduce per-unit variable costs versus maintaining a variable-cost-heavy structure that flexes with volume. Fixed costs create economies of scale when volume is high but create risk when volume is low.
concepts.context: Central to the Fonderia case: replacing 12 workers (variable cost) with one machine operator (fixed cost via capital investment in machinery).

concepts.name: Economies of Scale
concepts.definition: The cost advantage achieved when high volume production spreads fixed costs over more units, reducing per-unit cost. Only realized when sales volume is sufficiently high.
concepts.context: Professor explained that the Fonderia machine investment assumes sufficient economies of scale - if sales materialize, marginal cost per unit is very low; if not, the fixed investment is a burden.

concepts.name: Operating Leverage and Company Lifecycle Stage
concepts.definition: The appropriateness of operating leverage depends on the company's lifecycle stage. Growth-stage companies expecting output increases benefit from reducing variable costs (high operating leverage). Mature companies with stable/uncertain demand may prefer variable cost structures for flexibility.
concepts.context: Student Arush connected operating leverage to lifecycle stages, and the professor praised this connection as linking to course themes.

concepts.name: Contribution Margin
concepts.definition: Revenue minus variable costs; when divided by EBIT, it provides a measure of operating leverage.
concepts.context: Mentioned by a student (Ada/Ricardo) as the formula for operating leverage: contribution margin divided by EBIT.

concepts.name: Operating vs. Finance Lease Accounting (IFRS 16)
concepts.definition: Under the 2019 IFRS 16 rules, both operating and finance leases must be recognized on the balance sheet as a right-of-use asset and a corresponding liability. Previously, operating leases were treated as off-balance-sheet expenses. Now the lease payment is split into a depreciation component (on the asset) and an interest component (on the liability), rather than being a single operating expense.
concepts.context: Discussed as the foundational concept for the session, arising from pre-class reading on structural shifts in lease accounting standards.

concepts.name: Front-loaded Interest on Finance Leases
concepts.definition: With a finance lease treated as debt, interest payments are highest in year one (calculated on the full principal) and decrease over time as principal is paid down, similar to a mortgage. This means the tax shield from interest is front-loaded compared to the straight-line expense pattern of an operating lease.
concepts.context: A student asked why lease-related costs are higher at the beginning and decrease over time. Professor explained the analogy to a mortgage where outstanding principal shrinks with each payment.

concepts.name: Income Statement Differences: Operating vs. Finance Lease
concepts.definition: Under US GAAP, operating lease expenses are included in EBIT (as an operating expense), while finance lease interest is subtracted below EBIT. This means a finance lease makes operating profitability (EBIT/EBITDA) look better, even though net income effects are ultimately similar.
concepts.context: Student Reilly raised the point about how classification affects profitability metrics and equity ratios. Professor confirmed the different income statement placement under GAAP.

concepts.name: Lease vs. Buy NPV Analysis
concepts.definition: Comparing the net present value of cash flows under a leasing scenario vs. a buying/owning scenario for the same equipment. Key cash flows for owning include purchase price, maintenance costs, depreciation tax shields, and residual/sale value. Key cash flows for leasing include periodic lease payments and their associated tax effects.
concepts.context: Central pre-class exercise where students built a valuation model comparing the cost of leasing vs. owning equipment with an 8-year useful life over a 4-year horizon.

concepts.name: Discount Rate for Lease vs. Buy Analysis
concepts.definition: Because lease payments and debt payments are fixed contractual obligations with no operational risk, the appropriate discount rate is the after-tax cost of debt, not WACC or cost of equity. Using a higher discount rate (e.g., cost of equity) would distort the comparison by making owning appear artificially less attractive due to heavier discounting of future tax shields and sale proceeds.
concepts.context: Andrel raised a provocative question about whether equity financing of the purchase should change the discount rate. Professor demonstrated numerically that using 12% cost of equity instead of after-tax cost of debt made owning look worse, but argued this was misleading since the operational reality doesn't change.

concepts.name: Technology Obsolescence Risk in Lease vs. Buy
concepts.definition: When equipment becomes technologically obsolete quickly, leasing transfers the obsolescence risk to the lessor. However, in an efficient market, the lessor will price this risk into the lease payments. The advantage depends on whether the lessor has comparative advantages (e.g., economies of scale in servicing, access to upgrades, manufacturer affiliation) that reduce the cost of managing obsolescence.
concepts.context: Professor posed this as a specific scenario for discussion. Students debated whether lessors would simply charge a premium for fast-obsoleting equipment, and whether captive leasing companies might have structural advantages.

concepts.name: Sale-Leaseback Transactions
concepts.definition: A company sells an owned asset (e.g., a warehouse) to a buyer and simultaneously leases it back. This releases liquidity (cash from the sale) even though under new accounting rules the balance sheet impact is minimal (asset and liability remain). Useful for companies facing liquidity constraints or difficulty accessing credit markets.
concepts.context: Student Bochen asked how sale-leasebacks work in reality. Professor explained the mechanics and noted it is essentially a form of secured borrowing. Ann connected this to the WeWork case from the previous session.

concepts.name: Leasing for Non-Profit Organizations
concepts.definition: Non-profits do not pay taxes, so they cannot benefit from depreciation tax shields that come with ownership. This makes leasing relatively more attractive. Additionally, smaller non-profits may have difficulty borrowing money, making leasing a more accessible option. There is potential for negotiated deals where the lessor captures the tax shield and passes some savings through to the non-profit lessee.
concepts.context: Professor asked Dexter specifically about non-profit implications. Donato suggested a gentleman's agreement to share the tax shield benefit between lessor and non-profit lessee.

concepts.name: Captive Leasing Companies
concepts.definition: Leasing companies affiliated with manufacturers that may have comparative advantages in servicing, upgrading, and replacing equipment. They can position themselves as offering a service (e.g., 'transportation' rather than 'car leasing') which enables innovation and management of technological obsolescence.
concepts.context: Sonia brought up captive leasing companies and connected to B110/B113 concepts about companies defining their business by the service they provide rather than the product (e.g., rail companies failing by not seeing themselves as transportation companies).

concepts.name: Credit Rating Impact of Lease Capitalization
concepts.definition: Even before the 2019 accounting rule changes, sophisticated analysts and credit rating agencies were already capitalizing long-running operating leases onto the balance sheet for their assessments. Therefore, the change in accounting rules did not materially affect credit ratings.
concepts.context: Reilly asked whether the new accounting treatment (recognizing leases as debt) affects a company's credit rating. Professor explained that rating agencies were already doing this adjustment.

concepts.name: Useful Life and Break-Even on Ownership
concepts.definition: The financial comparison between leasing and owning is highly sensitive to the actual useful life of the asset. If the asset can be used beyond the initially assumed period, each additional year of ownership is essentially free (no lease payment), dramatically improving the economics of buying. Conversely, a shorter-than-expected useful life worsens the buy case.
concepts.context: Jakub noted that if the equipment could last 5+ years instead of 4, owning becomes much cheaper since year 5 has minimal cost (just maintenance). Professor agreed and emphasized the probability distribution of useful life as a key consideration.

concepts.name: Short-term Lease Exemptions and Gaming of Accounting Rules
concepts.definition: Leases under 12 months may be exempt from capitalization under the new rules. This creates potential incentives for companies to structure leases as rolling short-term contracts (e.g., annual renewals) to keep them off the balance sheet, especially if they have debt ratio covenants.
concepts.context: Ann asked whether a warehouse leased on annually renewed one-year contracts would escape the new capitalization rules. Professor noted this is a potential gaming strategy but was unsure of the specific regulatory details, and encouraged students to research it.

concepts.name: Forward Contract
concepts.definition: A private agreement obligating two parties to trade a particular asset (currency) at a specified price at a future date. Unlike options, both parties are obligated to execute the trade.
concepts.context: Introduced at the start of class as the foundational hedging instrument; professor asked students to define it and explain its benefits.

concepts.name: Futures Contract
concepts.definition: A standardized contract traded on an exchange obligating parties to trade a currency at a set price in the future. Differs from forwards in that it is publicly traded, standardized in size and maturity, and more liquid.
concepts.context: Discussed in contrast to forward contracts; professor explored upsides (liquidity, ability to exit) and downsides (lack of flexibility, standardized sizes/maturities, not available for all currencies).

concepts.name: Options Contract
concepts.definition: A contract giving the holder the right but not the obligation to execute a trade at a specified price. Briefly distinguished from forwards/futures where execution is mandatory.
concepts.context: Yiran initially confused futures with options; professor corrected and noted options would be discussed later in the course.

concepts.name: Hedging Rationale
concepts.definition: The purpose of hedging is to reduce uncertainty in future cash flows, not necessarily to increase profitability. It allows companies to plan operations with less risk and focus on core business rather than currency speculation.
concepts.context: Discussed as the fundamental motivation for using forward/futures contracts; professor emphasized that companies are not in the business of speculating on currencies.

concepts.name: Market Maker Spread
concepts.definition: Banks acting as market makers in forward contracts profit by offering slightly different buy and sell prices (the spread), while ideally maintaining a risk-neutral position by offsetting transactions.
concepts.context: Yasseri asked how the counterparty in a forward contract makes money; professor explained the bank's spread mechanism and offsetting transactions.

concepts.name: Financial Hedging
concepts.definition: Using financial instruments (forwards, futures, options) to reduce exposure to currency risk. Most useful for clearly defined, short-term exposures. Faster, more flexible, and easier to resize than operational hedging.
concepts.context: Contrasted with operational hedging in a prep poll discussion; students identified it as simpler, requiring less expertise, and more suitable for short-term risk.

concepts.name: Operational Hedging
concepts.definition: Reducing currency exposure through operational decisions such as moving production to the country where revenues are generated, so that costs and revenues move together with exchange rates. Addresses long-term competitive positioning but requires large upfront investment.
concepts.context: Central discussion topic comparing it to financial hedging; students identified benefits (long-term protection, competitive positioning) and downsides (CAPEX, irreversibility, quality control, political risk, execution risk, brand image issues).

concepts.name: Risk Shifting
concepts.definition: An operational hedge strategy where a company invoices customers or subsidiaries in its home currency, shifting currency risk to the counterparty. Effectiveness depends on bargaining power and market positioning.
concepts.context: Discussed as one of several operational hedging strategies; Yuran explained how companies with bargaining power can bill in home currency.

concepts.name: Leading and Lagging
concepts.definition: Timing strategy where payments or currency conversions are accelerated (leading) or delayed (lagging) depending on expected currency movements to take advantage of favorable exchange rates.
concepts.context: Discussed as an operational hedging technique; Tanmay explained the concept and the Airbus/United Airlines example was connected as a combination of lagging and risk shifting.

concepts.name: Plant Locations as Real Options
concepts.definition: Having production facilities in multiple countries creates real options to shift production to whichever location is cheaper due to currency movements, provided plants have spare capacity.
concepts.context: Discussed as an operational hedge; Ondra explained shifting production between US and European plants based on cost, and Sonia gave the Minerva residence example of geographic diversification averaging out costs.

concepts.name: Efficient Market Hypothesis in Forex
concepts.definition: In an efficient currency market, forward rates should on average equal future spot rates, meaning companies should not systematically gain or lose by hedging. If forward rates were consistently biased, traders would arbitrage until corrected.
concepts.context: Jakub raised the point that in efficient markets the difference between forward and spot rates should not be large; professor confirmed and used it to explore why companies still hedge.

concepts.name: Money Market Hedge
concepts.definition: A hedging technique where a company uses current cash to buy foreign currency today, invests it at the foreign interest rate, and uses the proceeds to pay a future foreign-currency payable, thereby locking in the exchange rate without using forwards/futures.
concepts.context: Discussed during pre-class work review; Daniya asked about the cost calculation and whether borrowing costs were embedded; professor clarified the present value discounting approach.

concepts.name: Competitive Position and Exchange Rates
concepts.definition: Currency fluctuations affect a company's competitive position by changing relative costs and revenues versus competitors. Even companies without direct currency exposure can be affected if competitors have exposure that changes their pricing.
concepts.context: Discussed in post-breakout session; Donato explained the cost/revenue impact, and Ada added that competitors' currency exposure indirectly affects your competitive position.

concepts.name: Currency Hedging Decision-Making (CFO Role)
concepts.definition: Currency hedging decisions fall under the expanding scope of the CFO or their team. Larger companies may have dedicated personnel for currency risk analysis, making the marginal cost of hedging low once infrastructure is in place.
concepts.context: Boqian asked who makes hedging decisions in a company; Sonia and Jakub discussed the CFO role, company size considerations, and the importance of post-hedge audit/analysis.

concepts.name: Natural Hedge via Foreign-Currency Borrowing
concepts.definition: Borrowing in a foreign currency to match foreign-currency revenues creates a natural hedge without requiring foreign operations. Euro revenues can service euro-denominated debt, reducing currency volatility impact.
concepts.context: Professor explained as an example of a less effort-intensive operational hedge that doesn't require moving operations abroad.

concepts.name: Interest Rate Quoting Conventions (APR vs APY)
concepts.definition: Interest rates can be quoted as APR (simple multiplication of periodic rate times periods) or as effective annual rate (APY). Understanding the quoting convention is essential for precise hedging calculations.
concepts.context: A student asked about converting a three-month annual interest rate; professor explained the distinction between APR and APY and the importance of knowing the quoting convention.

concepts.name: Exchange Rate Determinants
concepts.definition: The set of macroeconomic and political factors that drive current and expected future exchange rates, including inflation differentials, interest rate differentials, trade balances, GDP growth, government policy, political stability, government debt, risk perceptions, and global events
concepts.context: Opening discussion before the purple activity; professor solicited factors from multiple students to build a comprehensive picture

concepts.name: Withholding Tax on Repatriation
concepts.definition: Tax charged by a foreign country when profits are remitted (transferred) from a subsidiary back to the parent company in the home country; acts as a barrier to moving cash across borders
concepts.context: Dexter asked why subsidiaries must transfer profits back to parent companies; led to extended discussion on repatriation mechanics and tax avoidance strategies

concepts.name: Transfer Pricing Manipulation
concepts.definition: Practice of adjusting prices charged between related entities (parent and subsidiary) for goods, services, or IP licenses to shift profits to lower-tax jurisdictions; regulators require fair market value pricing to prevent abuse
concepts.context: Jakub raised the strategy of licensing IP to a subsidiary to extract profits without triggering withholding taxes; professor explained the cat-and-mouse game with regulators

concepts.name: Remittance Restrictions / Capital Controls
concepts.definition: Government policies that restrict the flow of money out of a country, used to prop up the local currency by reducing selling pressure; can include monitoring international wire transfers and imposing withdrawal taxes
concepts.context: Discussion of how Argentina and other countries restrict capital outflows; professor explained that withholding taxes are themselves a tool to affect exchange rates

concepts.name: Political Risk in International Investment
concepts.definition: Risk arising from political instability, changes in government policy, weak rule of law, expropriation/nationalization, and geopolitical tensions that can destroy or erode investment value
concepts.context: Multiple students discussed examples including Egypt 2011, Russian nationalization of Finnish factories, and unpredictable US trade policy; professor emphasized it varies by business type and home-country relations

concepts.name: Operational / Cultural Risk
concepts.definition: Risks from differences in business practices, consumer behavior, labor markets, and managerial culture in a foreign country that can cause actual cash flows to diverge from financial projections
concepts.context: Denia raised that strong financials on paper can deteriorate due to cultural and managerial discrepancies; Arush added risk of international cannibalization of existing exports

concepts.name: Currency Convertibility Risk
concepts.definition: Risk that a foreign currency may not be easily convertible to the home currency due to illiquid or non-functional foreign exchange markets, particularly in developing economies
concepts.context: Tanaswa raised that many African currencies cannot be readily converted to USD or GBP; professor agreed this requires creative financial solutions

concepts.name: Risk Spectrum of International Operations
concepts.definition: International business risk exists on a spectrum: simple exporting carries less risk than establishing brick-and-mortar operations with high capex; even software faces IP theft and regulatory bans
concepts.context: Professor synthesized student comments about how the type of business (software vs. factory) changes the nature and magnitude of risk, though no business is completely risk-free internationally

concepts.name: Interest Rate Parity / No-Arbitrage Condition
concepts.definition: In efficient markets, forward exchange rates should exactly offset interest rate differentials between two currencies, preventing risk-free arbitrage from borrowing in one currency and investing in another
concepts.context: Professor explained why the purple activity's projected appreciation of the Brazilian real was unrealistic given Brazil's 14% interest rate; forward rates should reflect depreciation to offset the interest differential

concepts.name: Natural Hedge via Local Currency Debt
concepts.definition: Borrowing in the same currency as the subsidiary's revenues reduces foreign exchange risk because if the local currency depreciates, both revenues and debt obligations decline together, reducing net exposure
concepts.context: Core concept in the purple activity comparing all-equity, USD debt, and BRL debt financing for a Brazilian subsidiary; Jakub and Dexter explained the hedging logic

concepts.name: APV (Adjusted Present Value) for Foreign Investment
concepts.definition: Valuation method that separates unlevered project value from financing effects (tax shields, financial distress costs); in multinational context, must also account for differences in tax shield depending on where debt is located (subsidiary vs. parent) and the natural hedging effect of local currency debt on the equity beta
concepts.context: Third question of the purple activity; professor walked through how APV handles the three cases (all equity, USD debt, BRL debt) and how the all-equity discount rate should be adjusted downward when local currency debt reduces FX risk

concepts.name: Tax Shield Location Effects
