cases.name: Target Corporation
cases.context: Central case study. Five capital investment projects evaluated by Target's CFO and capital expenditure committee. The company had a flattening stock price and was deciding how to allocate constrained capital across new store openings and remodels.
key_decisions: Which of five proposed store projects to fund with limited capital ($100M remaining of $300M budget)
How to balance financial metrics (NPV, IRR) against strategic/qualitative factors (brand visibility, competitive pre-emption)
Whether to invest in high-visibility urban locations with lower financial returns vs. safer suburban/rural locations
Whether the capital expenditure committee governance structure is effective
key_takeaways: In reality, Target accepted The Barn, Gopher Place, and Whelan Court; rejected Goldie Square and Stadium Remodel
The Barn was the strongest project: highest IRR (16%), highest NPV, zero cannibalization, large sales buffer (18% decline tolerance), low investment required ($13M)
Goldie Square was the weakest: IRR below WACC on retail sales alone, only positive NPV due to credit card revenue at 4% discount rate, lowest NPV overall, very sensitive to sales changes
Stadium Remodel was rejected because the company didn't want to set a precedent for large remodeling budgets; a smaller remodel was done later
Capital constraints fundamentally change project selection logic from maximizing total NPV to maximizing NPV per dollar invested

cases.name: APV Perpetuity Project (Hypothetical Scenario)
cases.context: Two-scenario analysis: Scenario 1 is all-equity financed project with $1.2M annual perpetuity cash flows discounted at 10% = $12M value. Scenario 2 adds $10M debt at 5% interest, 40% tax rate, increasing required equity return to 15%, with tax shield adding $4M-$6M in value for total of ~$16M.
key_decisions: Whether to finance with all-equity or use leverage

key_takeaways: APV breaks down value into operational component and financing component
Access to cheap debt financing is itself a source of firm value
Tax shield value = Tax Rate x Debt (when perpetual)
Missing component in simple model: financial distress costs

cases.name: Replacement Decision Pre-Classwork (Machinery Replacement)
cases.context: Spreadsheet exercise evaluating whether to replace old machinery with new equipment costing ~$480K. Old machine has $120K book value, $70K market value. New machine uses MACRS depreciation. Operating expenses decrease by $120K/year. Conflicting instructions about working capital changes.
key_decisions: Whether to replace existing machinery given incremental cash flow analysis
How to handle working capital changes (inventories and accounts payable)
MACRS vs. straight-line depreciation impact on project value
Break-even price of new machine (non-linear due to depreciation feedback)
key_takeaways: Must focus on incremental cash flows, not total
Tax-adjusted sale of old equipment includes tax benefit from recognized loss
MACRS front-loads depreciation for better tax shield timing
Break-even analysis is non-linear: higher cost means more depreciation tax shield
NPV was approximately $65K-$80K; break-even price around $545K-$570K

cases.name: Coca-Cola (Brief Reference)
cases.context: Bo Chen used as example of a stable company that could use cheap debt to increase valuation through APV framework. Professor confirmed that stable cash flows enable access to cheap debt, which is a source of value for acquirers.
key_takeaways: Stable companies can access cheaper debt financing
Access to cheap financing is part of acquisition value
Good capital structure increases overall firm valuation

cases.name: Aurora Textile Company
cases.context: US textile manufacturer facing intense international competition, considering an $8.25 million investment in a new Zinsser spinning machine to replace aging equipment. The company has declining revenues, negative net earnings, a BB credit rating, and only $2 million in cash.
cases.stage: Declining / Distressed mature company in a declining US industry
key_decisions: Whether to invest $8.25 million in a new Zinsser spinning machine
Whether to continue with status quo equipment for 10 more years
How to finance the investment given only $2M cash and BB credit rating
Whether to wait and preserve option value vs. invest now (machine price increases 5% per year)
key_takeaways: A positive NPV on paper does not guarantee a good investment if the company may not survive long enough to realize the cash flows
The company actually invested in the new machinery and filed for bankruptcy within two years
Credit constraints and liquidity concerns can override positive NPV analysis
Assumptions about inventory savings, revenue growth, and defect rates are highly uncertain and drive the model
Opportunity cost and option value of preserving cash must be weighed against project returns
Sensitivity analysis is essential when margins are thin and the industry is declining
Management track record matters: past poor decisions raise questions about future execution

cases.name: Philips
cases.context: Referenced from the readings as an example of a company that developed non-traditional or custom metrics for evaluating IT/technology investments
key_decisions: Developed custom metrics beyond DCF for investment evaluation
key_takeaways: Large mature companies may need to adapt evaluation frameworks to capture value of technology investments

cases.name: Hypothetical Software System Upgrade (Pre-classwork Scenarios)
cases.context: Students modeled two scenarios for a software system update: Scenario 1 (IT cost reduction only) with NPV of $4,600 and Scenario 2 (price decrease to sell more units) with NPV of $3,700
key_decisions: Whether to invest $12,000 in IT system upgrade
Scenario 1: Rely on 2% IT cost savings
Scenario 2: Reduce price by $1 to increase units sold by 5%/1,500 units
Choice of discount rate (10% WACC)
key_takeaways: Cost reduction assumptions are the primary value driver and must be scrutinized
Price elasticity assumptions heavily influence scenario 2 outcomes
Baseline comparison choice (status quo vs. incremental) affects NPV but not ranking
IT cost reduction estimates are often overly optimistic due to champion bias
Scenario 1 is superior to Scenario 2 in both comparison approaches

cases.name: Hypothetical IT Investment Approval Process (Role-Play Exercise)
cases.context: Students evaluated a proposed IT investment evaluation methodology with approval thresholds ($5K, $10K, $50K), CIO/CFO roles, and post-audit requirements through CEO/CIO role-play
key_decisions: Setting appropriate dollar thresholds for approval levels
Designing post-audit as improvement tool vs. punishment system
Balancing CFO oversight with operational agility
Preventing gaming through project splitting
key_takeaways: Thresholds should be benchmarked against industry practices
Post-audits should focus on estimation accuracy improvement, not punishment
Too much process kills innovation; company culture determines optimal process level
Risk management (cybersecurity, data privacy) was a missing element
Time-sensitive projects need fast-track mechanisms
Cross-departmental alignment of IT systems is important
Breaking large projects into small ones can be seen as creative (real options) rather than gaming
Power dynamics between CIO and CFO can politicize the process

cases.name: Sonia's Non-Profit Organization (Food Waste Reduction)
cases.context: Sonia shared her experience at a non-profit weighing a software decision, evaluating both financial cost and mission-related KPIs (reducing food waste, routing food better)
key_decisions: Chose higher-cost software because it better accomplished their food waste reduction mission
key_takeaways: Non-profits must combine financial metrics with mission-alignment KPIs
A more expensive option can be the right choice when it better serves organizational purpose

cases.name: Fonderia di Torino
cases.context: Italian foundry case involving a project valuation exercise for improving operational efficiency. The company is deciding whether to invest in a new automated machine to replace labor-intensive production. The new machine has higher fixed costs but lower variable costs (one operator vs. 12 workers). The case involves strong labor unions that affect labor costs and layoff packages.
key_decisions: Whether to invest in a new automated machine with high fixed costs
How to handle labor union constraints on layoffs and severance packages
Evaluating the trade-off between fixed machinery costs and variable labor costs
key_takeaways: Increasing operating leverage through capital investment is a double-edged sword
Must consider whether sales volume will be sufficient to justify the fixed cost investment
Labor union constraints add complexity to restructuring decisions
The investment fundamentally changes the company's operating leverage profile

cases.name: WeWork
cases.context: Used as a cautionary example by student Anne to illustrate the dangers of high operating leverage. WeWork invested heavily in buildings (fixed costs) globally, assuming high sales volume. When their pricing model failed to attract sufficient customers, they could not cover fixed costs (leases, mortgages) and filed for bankruptcy.
key_decisions: Massive investment in fixed-cost real estate globally
Freemium pricing model to drive volume
Attempted pricing changes that failed to generate sufficient revenue
key_takeaways: High operating leverage without corresponding sales volume leads to bankruptcy risk
Fixed cost commitments (leases, mortgages) cannot be easily reduced when demand falls short
Growth-stage companies taking on high fixed costs face existential risk if projections are wrong

cases.name: Pre-class Equipment Lease vs. Buy Model
cases.context: Hypothetical scenario comparing leasing equipment at $57,000/year vs. purchasing for $200,000 with 100% debt financing. Equipment has 8-year useful life but is analyzed over 4 years. Residual value drops to $20,000 after 4 years. Includes depreciation tax shields, maintenance costs, and after-tax cost of debt as the discount rate.
key_decisions: Whether to lease or buy equipment given a 4-year planning horizon
What discount rate to use for comparing the two options
Whether to extend ownership beyond 4 years for additional cost savings
key_takeaways: Headline NPV numbers favored leasing over the 4-year period
After-tax cost of debt is the appropriate discount rate for fixed contractual obligations
Extending ownership by even one year dramatically improves the buy case
Non-financial factors (flexibility, obsolescence risk, switching costs) must also be considered

cases.name: WeWork
cases.context: Referenced briefly by Ann in the context of sale-leaseback transactions. Connected to previous session's discussion about WeWork's financial distress. Ann asked whether WeWork could use sale-leasebacks to release cash for debt obligations.
key_decisions: Whether to sell owned properties and lease them back to improve liquidity
key_takeaways: Sale-leasebacks improve liquidity even if balance sheet impact is neutral under new rules
Strategy makes sense when company faces liquidity constraints

cases.name: Rolls Royce (as leasing example)
cases.context: Referenced by Sonia as an example of a captive leasing company that positions itself as providing a service (transportation/propulsion) rather than just leasing products, enabling innovation past technological obsolescence.
key_takeaways: Companies that define themselves by the service they provide rather than the product can better manage obsolescence

cases.name: Toyota Assignment (Assignment 1)
cases.context: Brief Q&A at the start of class about the assignment. Professor emphasized backing arguments with precise quantitative data, comparing with competitors like Honda using 2011 financial statements, and supporting strategic recommendations with industry statistics.
key_takeaways: External data is permitted and encouraged for comparisons
Arguments must be supported with precise, ideally quantitative data
End notes vs. footnotes: either format acceptable
Word count flexibility: modest overages acceptable; end notes and tables don't count

cases.name: Toyota
cases.context: Referenced by Anne as an example of quality control challenges when moving production to different countries as part of operational hedging.
key_decisions: Moving production internationally
key_takeaways: Quality control is a major risk when operationally hedging through production relocation

cases.name: TSMC
cases.context: Referenced by Anne regarding concerns about worker quality at the Arizona fab compared to Taiwan operations.
key_decisions: Building fabrication facility in the US (Arizona)
key_takeaways: Worker quality and operational standards may differ across locations, undermining operational hedging benefits

cases.name: United Airlines / Airbus
cases.context: David described United Airlines purchasing Airbus A350 planes in 2009 but repeatedly delaying delivery until ~2030, locking in a favorable price. Professor characterized this as a combination of risk shifting and lagging.
key_decisions: Placing aircraft order with delayed delivery to lock in favorable pricing
key_takeaways: Creative contract structuring can function as an operational hedge
Depends on counterparty's willingness to accept risk in the contract terms

cases.name: Minerva University
cases.context: Sonia used Minerva's multi-city residence model as an example of how geographic diversification of locations can average out costs (housing costs across cities).
key_decisions: Operating residences in multiple cities globally
key_takeaways: Geographic diversification can serve as a real option to average out or reduce costs

cases.name: German Beer Producers
cases.context: Referenced by Anne as an example where producing outside Germany would undermine the brand image and quality perception, limiting operational hedging options.
key_takeaways: Brand identity tied to country of origin limits operational hedging through production relocation

cases.name: Brazilian Subsidiary Investment (Purple Activity)
cases.context: Hypothetical case of a US parent company evaluating a 20 million BRL investment in a Brazilian subsidiary; modeled projected exchange rates (with unrealistic BRL appreciation), local BRL debt financing, withholding taxes, and salvage value
key_decisions: Whether to finance with all equity, USD debt, or BRL debt
Whether to borrow at the subsidiary or parent level
How to handle repatriation and withholding taxes
Whether and how to hedge currency exposure
How to model uncertain salvage value
key_takeaways: BRL debt provides a natural hedge against currency risk
Subsidiary-level debt shields against both corporate and withholding taxes
APV separates operational value from financing effects in multinational projects
Forward exchange rates should reflect interest rate differentials under no-arbitrage
Operational hedging (matching costs and debt to revenue currency) minimizes exposure before financial hedging

cases.name: Taiwanese Company with Chinese Subsidiary
cases.context: David shared a real example of a Taiwanese company that avoided remittance restrictions by purchasing equipment in China with the Chinese subsidiary and shipping it to Taiwan instead of transferring cash
key_decisions: Using physical goods transfers instead of cash remittance to avoid capital controls
key_takeaways: Creative non-cash solutions can circumvent remittance restrictions legally

cases.name: Finnish Companies in Russia
cases.context: Ada cited Finland losing factories in Russia due to nationalization after the Russian invasion of Ukraine, while Chinese companies continued operating normally
key_takeaways: Political risk varies depending on home-country geopolitical alignment with the host country
Nationalization/expropriation is a real risk in politically unstable environments

cases.name: Egypt 2011
cases.context: Tarideh cited Egypt's political instability in 2011 as an example where businesses had their profits eroded and had to exit within one to two years
key_takeaways: Political instability can cascade into exchange rate collapse and operational failure

cases.name: Tata (mentioned briefly)
cases.context: A student asked about the LV future assignment referencing Tata from B113; professor said to find a different mature diversified conglomerate if in India
key_takeaways: Cross-course connection: Tata studied in B113 as a diversified conglomerate example

cases.name: Tata Group
cases.context: Discussed as a large diversified Indian conglomerate with global presence through TCS, Jaguar Land Rover, and Tata food/beverage brands. Used as basis for an LBA assignment where students find comparable companies in their rotation cities.
key_decisions: Diversification across many industries (consultancy, automotive, food/beverage)
Global expansion through acquisitions like Jaguar Land Rover
key_takeaways: Large conglomerates can achieve global diversification across multiple product lines
TCS provides significant global presence
Used as benchmark for assignment comparing diversified companies across countries

cases.name: General Electric (GE)
cases.context: Discussed as an extreme example of diversification gone wrong. Expanded into aviation, healthcare, financial services, appliances, beauty products. Eventually split into three separate firms.
key_decisions: Extensive unrelated diversification across many industries
Ultimate decision to split into three distinct firms
key_takeaways: There are limits to managerial oversight when diversification becomes too broad
Poor management and poorly-timed acquisitions contributed to failure
Example of diversification for expansion's sake without proper value creation analysis

cases.name: Marriott
cases.context: Classified during breakout as focused in hospitality with high global diversification. Students debated whether hotels and real estate are the same industry.
key_decisions: Stayed focused on hospitality/travel services
key_takeaways: Vulnerability to unexpected events like COVID due to industry concentration
Hotel chains often own significant real estate assets which could be a diversification angle
Related diversification into real estate is debatable as same vs. different industry

cases.name: Toyota
cases.context: Classified as related diversification with high global market reach. Students were surprised it remained mostly in automotive rather than diversifying like Korean chaebols.
key_decisions: Remained focused on automotive industry
Toyota Financial Services exists but confined to car loans
key_takeaways: Despite expertise in efficient manufacturing, has not diversified into other manufacturing sectors
Contrasts with Korean chaebols (Samsung, Hyundai) and Japanese keiretsu (Mitsubishi) that diversified more broadly

cases.name: Lego
cases.context: Discussed as example of related diversification (bricks, amusement parks, movies, robotics). Students debated whether amusement parks count as unrelated. Past case from core courses referenced.
key_decisions: Expanded into amusement parks, movies, robotics
Pulled back from some unrelated ventures after poor returns
Created customer feedback business units
key_takeaways: Diversification into products customers do not want destroys value
Customer feedback integration is essential for successful diversification
Lego movies represent completely different revenue source that worked

cases.name: Carlsberg
cases.context: Identified as focused primarily on beer. Students discussed potential diversification into food/dining/entertainment sector.
key_decisions: Remained focused on beer
key_takeaways: Adjacent diversification opportunities exist (food, dining, entertainment)
Beer as a product has natural connections to adjacent industries

cases.name: Danaher
cases.context: Briefly mentioned during breakout as a science and technology / biotech company. Classified as related diversification with global presence.

cases.name: Red Bull
cases.context: Ece brought up as example of diversification through extreme sports sponsorships and competitions. Ada pushed back that this is marketing rather than true operational diversification.
key_decisions: Sponsoring extreme sports competitions
key_takeaways: Fine line between marketing strategy and actual diversification
Sponsorships/competitions may not extend operations or generate separate revenue streams

cases.name: Bridgestone and Firestone
cases.context: Riri referenced from core courses as an example of a diversification/merger that worked well due to synergies.
key_decisions: Merger between tire companies
key_takeaways: Synergy-driven mergers can create value
Individual case outcomes do not generalize to whether diversification is universally good or bad

cases.name: Snapple (acquired by Quaker Oats)
cases.context: Riri referenced from core courses as an example of a diversification/acquisition that failed despite cultural similarities, due to management issues.
key_decisions: Acquisition by Quaker Oats
key_takeaways: Management quality matters more than surface-level compatibility
For every successful diversification case, there is a counterexample of failure

cases.name: Techint Grupo (Tekint)
cases.context: Ada asked after class about using this Argentinian-Italian company as comparable to Tata for her LBA assignment. Related industries diversification plus expansion into medicine. Not publicly traded.
key_decisions: Diversification into related industries plus medicine
key_takeaways: Non-public companies have limited financial disclosure
Professor confirmed the assignment goal is finding any large diversified company, not necessarily close match to Tata
LBA can leverage rotation city knowledge for market opportunity analysis

cases.name: Unnamed Mature Company (Pre-classwork DCF Model)
cases.context: A mature company used in the pre-classwork spreadsheet model with 18% ROIC, ~16% reinvestment rate, ~3% growth rate, 20% debt-to-capital ratio (Scenario 1) and 40% debt-to-capital ratio (Scenario 2). Students decomposed value into existing assets vs. growth assets and modeled the effect of capital structure changes.
key_decisions: How much to reinvest vs. distribute to stakeholders
Whether to increase leverage from 20% to 40% debt-to-capital
Maintaining vs. growing the asset base
key_takeaways: Value of existing assets can be isolated using NOPAT/WACC perpetuity
Increasing debt from 20% to 40% increased firm value from 5162 to 5507
WACC decreased despite both cost of debt and cost of equity increasing
Capital structure changes also affect valuation multiples (P/E, EV/EBITDA)

cases.name: Tata Group (Assignment Reference)
cases.context: Referenced as an example of a large diversified mature company for the course assignment. Students are asked to pick a similar large mature company and propose an investment opportunity (could be an acquisition or new project).
key_decisions: What new investment opportunity to pursue
Whether to build or acquire
key_takeaways: Large diversified companies serve as good subjects for investment analysis
Students should justify assumptions with market research rather than arbitrary numbers
Acquisitions are valid project proposals if synergies or strategic fit can be demonstrated

cases.name: Microsoft
cases.context: Extended case study examining Microsoft's capital structure decisions from 2010 to present: debt ratio evolution from 0.1 (2010) to 1.25 (2017) and back down; LinkedIn and GitHub acquisitions financed with debt; triple-A credit rating maintained throughout; post-2017 equity growth driven by Azure/cloud success; professor argued Microsoft missed opportunity to borrow at near-zero rates in 2021-2022 to repurchase shares
key_decisions: Borrowed significantly to finance LinkedIn acquisition (~2016) and GitHub acquisition rather than repatriating foreign cash due to tax penalties
Did not increase borrowing after 2017 despite having capacity and declining debt ratio
Retained large earnings rather than paying them all out as dividends
Maintained stock buyback program but could not keep up with cash generation
key_takeaways: Theory (trade-off) says Microsoft should have more debt, but practice shows pecking order behavior prevailed
Microsoft maintained triple-A rating even at peak leverage, so the study's predicted downgrade was too conservative
Missed a significant value creation opportunity by not borrowing at near-zero rates in 2021-2022 to repurchase shares
Debt ratio declined naturally post-2017 due to massive equity growth from Azure/cloud profitability, not active deleveraging
Large companies have inertia in adjusting capital structure (steering a cruise ship vs. a small boat analogy)

cases.name: Toyota
cases.context: Discussed briefly at end of class in a Q&A about a prior assignment; focused on separating the automotive division from the financial services subsidiary (Toyota Credit Corporation) on the consolidated balance sheet
key_decisions: Operates a financial services subsidiary (Toyota Credit Corporation) that holds ~12 trillion yen in assets (finance receivables + operating leases)
Finance subsidiary operates like a bank with ~90% debt-to-asset ratio
key_takeaways: Consolidated leverage (~1.1 D/E) is misleading because most debt belongs to the finance subsidiary
Automotive division alone has very low leverage (~0.1-0.2 D/E), indicating significant unused debt capacity
Must separate finance receivables (current + non-current) and vehicles/equipment on operating leases to identify finance subsidiary assets
Finance subsidiary liabilities can be estimated as ~90% of its identified assets

cases.name: Hill Country Snack Foods
cases.context: Central case study: a conservative, all-equity snack food company considering recapitalization by taking on debt to repurchase shares. Students analyzed financial metrics at 0%, 20%, 40%, and 60% debt-to-capital ratios.
key_decisions: Whether to maintain all-equity structure or take on debt
How much debt to issue (20%, 40%, or 60% of capital)
Whether to use $55 million in excess cash plus borrowed funds for share repurchases
Balancing conservative corporate culture with shareholder value creation pressure
key_takeaways: All-equity structure provided stability and flexibility but left value on the table from unused debt capacity
EPS maximized at 40% debt, but this alone does not confirm value maximization
ROE/ROA comparison revealed very low leverage (equity multiplier ~1.25x) vs. competitors
Conservative operational strategy drove stable but slow growth (6-8%) independent of capital structure
Full APV analysis (tax shields + distress costs + unlevered value) needed to determine true optimal debt level
Debt rating would decline from ~AA at 20% to B at 60%, sharply increasing borrowing costs

cases.name: PepsiCo
cases.context: Competitor comparison; highly leveraged (equity multiplier ~4x), higher sales growth but net income growth lagging sales, strong market position.
key_takeaways: Most highly leveraged of the three competitors (ROE/ROA ratio ~4x)
Sales growth outpaced net income growth, suggesting high spending to achieve growth
Served as example of effective but aggressive capital structure

cases.name: Snyder's-Lance
cases.context: Competitor comparison; moderate leverage, aggressive acquisition strategy, high goodwill, payout ratio above 100%, unusually high P/E ratio.
cases.stage: Mature / acquisition-driven growth with possible distress signals
key_takeaways: Goodwill 3x Hill Country's, indicating heavy acquisition activity
Net income growth (2.3%) far below sales growth (18%), suggesting acquisitions not yet generating synergies
Payout ratio >100% unsustainable; likely caused by one-time charges depressing net income
High P/E likely reflected market expectation of future synergy realization or temporarily low earnings
Potentially heading toward financial trouble if trends continue

cases.name: Apple
cases.context: Referenced by Jakub as example of a company that borrows to buy back shares in mature/traditional industries.
key_takeaways: Used as real-world precedent for debt-funded share repurchase programs

cases.name: Toyota
cases.context: Referenced as an example of currency risk from concentration in one currency (yen); showed problems of being primarily based in one currency that moves unfavorably
key_decisions: Currency diversification through international operations
key_takeaways: Geographic concentration creates currency risk
Unfavorable currency movements can significantly hurt a company concentrated in one currency

cases.name: Tata Group
cases.context: Referenced in context of the course assignment; student (Daniela from Hyderabad) asked if she could use Tata Group since it was already referenced in the assignment; professor approved with guidance
key_takeaways: Used as a reference point for the final assignment's company analysis

cases.name: Hypothetical US Parent with Brazil/Thailand/Kenya Subsidiaries
cases.context: Activity 2 exercise where students calculated cost of equity for subsidiaries in Brazil, Thailand, and Kenya using Damodaran's country risk data
key_decisions: Determining appropriate discount rates per country
Whether to use same or different capital structures
key_takeaways: Each country requires customized cost of capital calculation
Default spreads and country risk premiums vary significantly
Cannot use a single discount rate across all foreign operations

cases.name: Berkshire Hathaway
cases.context: Used as example of a company with a no-dividend policy that retains all cash for investment; shareholder motions to pay dividends are consistently voted down because the clientele prefers capital gains and tax deferral
key_decisions: Retain all cash rather than pay dividends
Wait for good investment opportunities
key_takeaways: Established clientele effects are very powerful and self-reinforcing
Anti-dividend clienteles exist and actively defend the no-dividend policy

cases.name: Nokia (Finland)
cases.context: Example of Finnish companies that maintained high dividend payouts even after revenues stopped growing post-2008 financial crisis; Nokia had some good years after 2008 but never truly recovered
key_decisions: Maintained dividends at pre-crisis growth rates despite revenue stagnation
key_takeaways: Dividend stickiness can lead to persistent underinvestment at the aggregate economy level
Companies resist cutting dividends even when fundamentals deteriorate

cases.name: Polish State-Owned Enterprises
cases.context: Jakub described how majority of dividend-paying companies on Warsaw Stock Exchange are state-owned with poor financial results; private sector grows while SOEs stagnate
key_decisions: Pay dividends despite poor performance
key_takeaways: Dividends can be a negative signal when associated with companies lacking growth opportunities
State ownership can distort dividend policy rationale

cases.name: Microsoft / Google / Amazon
cases.context: Cited as examples of relatively old, mature companies that found significant new growth through AI, data centers, and cloud computing
key_takeaways: High revenue growth does not necessarily indicate a young company
Mature companies can re-enter growth phases through innovation or industry shifts

cases.name: Zimbabwe Economy / Currency Example
cases.context: Tanatsa shared personal experience with hyperinflation and currency instability; professor showed trillion-dollar Zimbabwean banknotes; example of extreme preference for immediate dividend payouts
key_takeaways: In unstable economies with high inflation, investors have extreme time preference for immediate cash via dividends
Currency instability and arbitrary government policy changes drive dividend preference

cases.name: Brazilian Market
cases.context: Ricardo described Brazil's historically untaxed dividends, high government bond yields (~14% on 10-year), and compensation for equity risk through stable dividend payouts
key_takeaways: Tax-free dividends create strong incentive for dividend distribution
High interest rates on government bonds mean equities must compensate through dividends to attract investors
Poor governance and information asymmetry reinforce dividend preference

cases.name: Minerva University
cases.context: Real-time institutional crisis: Minerva faces a ~$6M cash shortfall for the fall semester, leading to cancellation of the Berlin campus (which costs ~$2M at the top end). Funding sources include Nippon Foundation ($5M/year for 10 years), Reed Hastings ($10M/year for 10 years), and ad hoc donations. Administration made the decision without consulting faculty or students.
key_decisions: Administration cancelled Berlin campus to address part of the $6M shortfall
Students strategize about fundraising approaches: GoFundMe, Board of Trustees outreach, social media campaigns
Need to get concrete commitment from administration on timeline and dollar amount
key_takeaways: Stakeholder communication failures (no faculty/student consultation) erode trust and morale
Concentrated funding sources create vulnerability when shortfalls occur
Effective negotiation requires concrete data: specific timeline, specific dollar amount needed
Economies of scale matter for institutional sustainability as student body grows
Alumni engagement is an underutilized resource in early-stage institutions

cases.name: Company A (Low Credit Rating, Poor Investment Opportunities)
cases.context: Cash deployment analysis exercise. Firm has low credit rating and limited growth prospects.
key_decisions: Repay debt to reduce bankruptcy risk
key_takeaways: When default risk is high and growth is low, debt reduction takes priority over shareholder returns

cases.name: Company B (High Tobin's Q)
cases.context: Cash deployment analysis exercise. Firm has high market-to-replacement-cost ratio, suggesting valuable investment opportunities.
key_decisions: Invest cash in projects rather than return to shareholders
key_takeaways: High Tobin's Q signals the market believes the firm creates value through its investments; returning cash would waste that advantage

cases.name: Company C (Low Tobin's Q)
cases.context: Cash deployment analysis exercise. Market values the firm below replacement cost of assets.
key_decisions: Buy back shares since they may be undervalued
key_takeaways: Low Tobin's Q can indicate share undervaluation, making buybacks attractive because the firm acquires its own assets cheaply relative to replacement cost

cases.name: Company D (High Dividend Payout, Good Credit Rating)
cases.context: Cash deployment analysis exercise. Firm already pays high dividends and has access to cheap debt.
key_decisions: Use buybacks for one-time cash to avoid ratcheting dividend expectations
Could borrow at low rates to fund buybacks while deploying cash into investments
key_takeaways: Dividend increases create long-term commitments; buybacks offer flexibility for irregular surpluses
Good credit rating opens the option to borrow cheaply, separating financing from distribution decisions

cases.name: Reliance Industries / Jio
cases.context: Used as an example of an equity carve-out. Reliance took its Jio telecommunications subsidiary public, raising significant capital through an IPO of the subsidiary while retaining control.
key_decisions: Carved out Jio as a separate publicly traded entity
Raised external capital without giving up control of the subsidiary
Used the carve-out strategically rather than out of financial distress
key_takeaways: Equity carve-outs can unlock value trapped inside a conglomerate
A carve-out is primarily an asset-side restructuring tool
Proceeds can be deployed for growth or debt reduction depending on the parent's needs

cases.name: PE-Acquired Subsidiaries (Generic Example)
cases.context: Professor described a pattern where private equity firms acquire a company, load it with acquisition debt at the subsidiary level, and if the subsidiary fails, it declares bankruptcy while the PE firm's other holdings are shielded.
key_decisions: Push acquisition debt down to the subsidiary rather than holding it at the fund level
Structure the deal so that subsidiary bankruptcy does not cascade to the parent
key_takeaways: Covenant protections exist specifically because of this practice
Lenders must evaluate not just the borrower but the corporate structure above and below it
Debt placement within a corporate structure is itself a strategic decision

cases.name: Distressed Real Estate Sector (Current Environment)
cases.context: Referenced as a real-world example of interest rate driven distress. Approximately a trillion dollars of real estate loans originated during the COVID low-rate era are now maturing into a much higher rate environment, creating widespread refinancing risk.
key_decisions: Borrowers must decide whether to refinance at higher rates, sell assets, or negotiate with lenders
Lenders must decide whether to extend, modify, or foreclose
key_takeaways: External macro factors (interest rates) can trigger systemic restructuring needs
The problem is not necessarily bad assets but bad timing on debt maturity
Scale of distress can create market-wide fire sale conditions

cases.name: Oracle
cases.context: Referenced twice: (1) as an example of using AI to cut employee costs, illustrating the 'increase return' lever of ROIC improvement; (2) as a negative example of poor severance messaging during restructuring execution.

cases.name: Block (formerly Square)
cases.context: Cited as a positive example of generous severance during restructuring, contrasted with Oracle's poor messaging.

cases.name: Minerva (Berlin situation)
cases.context: Connected to the principle that acting too late in restructuring limits options. Minerva's Berlin experience served as a cautionary example of delayed action.

cases.name: Disney / Jimmy Kimmel
cases.context: Referenced as an example of restructuring backlash. Cancellation decisions that were poorly received publicly.

cases.name: Hospital + Insurance Company
cases.context: Hypothetical or real example of a vertically integrated entity where the insurance arm and hospital arm have conflicting goals, motivating a spin-off.

cases.name: Rokoboro/Rockborough
cases.context: A company historically associated with machine tools that is attempting to reposition as a technology/software company, focusing on CAD/CAM products with an AWS/AI-driven pivot. Faces a critical competitive window with competitors launching similar products within a year. The company has historically been debt-averse and must decide on a dividend policy during this strategic transition.
key_decisions: What dividend policy to adopt: 0%, residual, 10% + buybacks, or maintain historical 40% payout
How to fund aggressive R&D investment during AWS/AI critical period
How to signal strategic repositioning to investors while managing existing shareholder expectations
Whether to accept borrowing to maintain dividends despite historical debt aversion
key_takeaways: Industry peers (Autodesk, CAD companies) pay 0% dividends - precedent supports zero payout
Even optimistic projections show 40% payout creates $150M+ borrowing needs, contradicting debt-averse philosophy
Company is confused about its identity (software vs. machine tools) which affects appropriate benchmarks
Projections combine too many optimistic assumptions simultaneously (fast growth + margin improvement + international expansion + efficient working capital/capex)
International expansion contradicts low capex and working capital assumptions in projections
15% growth target appears optimistic versus competitor benchmarks
Interest expense is missing from the financial projections - a red flag

cases.name: Canadian Pacific (CP)
cases.context: Primary case study. A diversified Canadian conglomerate dating to the 1880s railroad era, with five business segments: CP Rail, CP Ships, PanCanadian Energy (oil/gas), Fording Coal, and Fairmont Hotels. Under CEO O'Brien, CP had already undergone significant operational restructuring but still faced a persistent 20-23% conglomerate discount.
key_decisions: O'Brien's operational restructuring: sold non-core businesses, moved HQ from Montreal to Calgary, used proceeds to pay down debt
Decision to pursue a full five-way starburst split rather than selective spin-offs or divestitures
Negotiated with Canadian government to change tax law allowing tax-free treatment of the split
Chose spin-off structure over divestiture to avoid capital gains tax
key_takeaways: Operational restructuring has limits when the problem is structural -- the conglomerate discount persisted despite tripling revenues
A starburst split captured roughly the full estimated conglomerate discount (20% on announcement, additional 15% post-split)
Political leverage as a large conglomerate was itself an asset -- used to secure favorable tax treatment before breaking up
External third-party valuation (Smith-Barney) provided credibility that management self-assessment could not

cases.name: Google / Alphabet (Comparison Discussion)
cases.context: Raised in discussion as a counterpoint to the CP conglomerate discount argument. Professor argued Google may not face the same discount because its divisions are related (Search and YouTube are both advertising businesses; Cloud leverages internal infrastructure).
key_takeaways: Not every conglomerate has a big conglomerate discount
The discount depends on whether divisions are truly unrelated or share meaningful synergies
Related diversification (ad businesses, shared tech infrastructure) is different from unrelated diversification (rail, hotels, oil)

frameworks.name: Company Lifecycle Stages (Seven Stages)
frameworks.description: Startup, growth, expansion, established, mature, decline (and potentially others). A stylized model for thinking about where a company is in its development and what financial decisions are appropriate.
frameworks.application: Students classified companies into stages and identified stage-appropriate financial decisions. Professor emphasized it's a model/thinking tool, not a rigid path.

frameworks.name: Pecking Order Theory
frameworks.description: Hierarchy of financing preferences: (1) internal funds, (2) debt, (3) equity. Companies prefer cheaper/less dilutive sources first.
frameworks.application: Applied to AstroTalk's financing decision: internal funds insufficient, debt too risky for barely profitable company, equity round chosen for valuation benchmark and capital.

frameworks.name: Porter's Five Forces (from B112)
frameworks.description: Buyer power, supplier power, threat of new entrants, threat of substitutes, rivalry among existing competitors.
frameworks.application: Mapped to DCF inputs: buyer power affects pricing/margins, supplier power affects COGS, competition affects SG&A and gross margins. Used to connect strategic analysis to financial projections.

frameworks.name: Institutional Voids (from B112)
frameworks.description: Weaknesses in institutional infrastructure (weak banks, lack of credit, non-independent markets) in certain markets that may present opportunities or risks.
frameworks.application: Debated whether currency instability is an institutional void (consensus: no, it's macroeconomic/policy). Discussed how institutional voids affect DCF assumptions and international financial analysis.

frameworks.name: Stress Testing Financial Decisions
frameworks.description: Evaluating a financial decision under changed assumptions: capital market access tightening, cost of capital rising, regulatory changes, competitive shifts, government intervention.
frameworks.application: Each group stress-tested their company's decision. E.g., SpaceX: if both equity and debt markets tighten, must curtail R&D. Netflix: sharp subscriber decline could force debt financing.

frameworks.name: Dominant Objective and Binding Constraint Analysis
frameworks.description: For any financial decision, identify the primary goal (dominant objective) and the key limiting factor (binding constraint) that shapes the decision.
frameworks.application: Applied to all five company breakout groups. E.g., Meta: objective is new revenue stream, constraint is competition and regulatory uncertainty.

frameworks.name: NPV/IRR Project Ranking Framework
frameworks.description: Standard DCF-based evaluation comparing projects on NPV and IRR, benchmarked against WACC as the hurdle rate.
frameworks.application: Applied to rank five Target store projects. The Barn ranked highest on both NPV and IRR. Goldie Square's IRR fell below WACC on retail alone.

frameworks.name: Capital Rationing / NPV per Dollar Invested
frameworks.description: When capital is constrained, maximize total value by selecting the combination of projects with the highest NPV-to-investment ratio rather than simply picking the highest absolute NPV project.
frameworks.application: With a $120M constraint, The Barn + Gopher Place (~$36M combined investment) generated more combined NPV ($73M) than Whelan Court alone (~$119M investment, ~$26M NPV).

frameworks.name: Sensitivity Analysis / Sales Buffer Framework
frameworks.description: Testing how much key assumptions can deteriorate before a project breaks even, providing a measure of downside risk protection.
frameworks.application: The Barn could tolerate an 18% sales decrease vs. Gopher Place's ~5% buffer. Used to assess risk-adjusted attractiveness of projects.

frameworks.name: Strategic vs. Financial Evaluation Matrix
frameworks.description: Multidimensional project evaluation considering both quantifiable financial metrics (NPV, IRR, sensitivity) and qualitative strategic factors (brand visibility, competitive positioning, market signaling, real options).
frameworks.application: Used throughout the session to argue for or against projects. Whelan Court had weak financial metrics but potential strategic upside; The Barn was financially strong but strategically limited.

frameworks.name: Capital Expenditure Committee Governance Framework
frameworks.description: A multi-functional committee structure for capital allocation decisions, with multiple approval layers and thresholds (e.g., board approval above $50M).
frameworks.application: Evaluated for effectiveness at Target. Discussed tradeoffs between thoroughness/rigor and speed/agility, and the risk of incentive misalignment and quantification bias.

frameworks.name: Differential Discount Rate Approach
frameworks.description: Applying different discount rates to cash flow streams with different risk profiles within the same project, rather than a single blended WACC.
frameworks.application: Goldie Square used 4% for predictable credit card revenues vs. 9% WACC for retail revenues, resulting in a positive blended NPV despite retail operations losing money.

frameworks.name: Act One / Act Two Growth Framework (McKinsey)
frameworks.description: Two-stage growth model: Act One focuses on core product optimization (monetization, pricing, leadership), Act Two involves branching into new markets, new channels, and adjacent products once primary market is saturated.
frameworks.application: Applied to Microsoft's evolution from Windows-centric (Act One) to diversified platform company with Azure, Office 365, gaming, AI, and acquisitions (Act Two). Also referenced as a strategic option for Scenario 2 companies facing declining SGR.

frameworks.name: Sustainable Growth Rate (SGR) Model
frameworks.description: SGR = ROE x Retention Ratio, where ROE is decomposed via DuPont (Profit Margin x Asset Turnover x Leverage). Represents how fast a firm can grow while keeping capital structure constant. Key correction: it is NOT growth without new debt -- it requires proportional new debt.
frameworks.application: Used in pre-class spreadsheet model (Scenario 1 vs Scenario 2) and industry data analysis. Scenario 1 maintains SGR by keeping leverage constant; Scenario 2 shows SGR declining when no new debt is taken. Industry data showed SGR consistently exceeding actual growth for mature companies.

frameworks.name: DuPont Analysis (as SGR component)
frameworks.description: Decomposition of ROE into Profit Margin x Asset Turnover x Financial Leverage. When any component declines (as in Scenario 2 where asset turnover and leverage both fall), ROE and therefore SGR decline.
frameworks.application: Used to explain mechanically why SGR declines in Scenario 2: asset turnover falls (sales lag assets), leverage falls (no new debt), leading to declining ROE.

frameworks.name: Positive NPV Threshold for Capital Allocation
frameworks.description: Retain earnings only when the company has positive NPV projects offering returns above cost of capital. Once exhausted, return cash to shareholders via dividends or buybacks. If company cannot produce required return on equity, investors are better off receiving cash back.
frameworks.application: Used to explain when mature companies should shift from retention to distribution, connecting to dividend policy for Scenario 2 companies and real examples like Apple, Microsoft, and Berkshire Hathaway.

frameworks.name: Two-Scenario Spreadsheet Growth Model
frameworks.description: Scenario 1: 100% retention, fixed capital structure (constant debt ratio), 25% sales growth. Scenario 2: 100% retention, no new debt (fixed liabilities), only 5% sales growth. Demonstrates how capital structure decisions interact with market-driven sales growth.
frameworks.application: Pre-class exercise showing that maintaining fixed leverage with matching sales growth keeps ratios stable (Scenario 1), while retaining earnings without matching sales growth leads to declining asset turnover, leverage, ROE, and SGR (Scenario 2).

frameworks.name: NPV / IRR / Payback Period Framework
frameworks.description: Capital budgeting decision tools reviewed from B111. NPV gives dollar value of project; IRR gives percentage return to compare against WACC; payback period measures time to recoup investment.
frameworks.application: Used as the theoretical benchmark against which actual CFO survey behavior was compared. Students analyzed why CFOs deviate from purely NPV/IRR-based decision making.

frameworks.name: Adjusted Present Value (APV)
frameworks.description: Separate the unlevered project value (discount at unlevered cost of capital) from the financing effects (tax shields, issuance costs). Add them together rather than incorporating debt into WACC.
frameworks.application: Introduced conceptually from the readings; professor previewed it as a topic for detailed study in upcoming sessions. Noted as procedurally simpler when debt levels fluctuate.

frameworks.name: Trade-Off Theory
frameworks.description: Firms should balance tax benefits of debt against costs of financial distress to find an optimal capital structure that minimizes WACC.
frameworks.application: Compared against the survey data showing CFOs prioritize flexibility over WACC optimization. Professor noted this is more applicable to larger firms with lower information costs.

frameworks.name: Pecking Order Theory
frameworks.description: Firms prefer internal funds first, then debt, then equity, due to information asymmetry costs. Not about optimizing capital structure but about minimizing adverse selection costs.
frameworks.application: Survey data showed real-world behavior aligns more with pecking order, especially for smaller firms. Convertible debt emerges as a practical compromise instrument.

frameworks.name: System Dynamics / Feedback Loop Analysis
frameworks.description: Analyzing how incentive structures create reinforcing feedback loops and time-delayed effects that can distort decision-making and inflate expectations.
frameworks.application: Applied to earnings targets: once introduced, they create reinforcing loops of inflated expectations. Connected to overestimated synergies from session 3. Professor emphasized careful incentive design to avoid distorting measured outcomes.

frameworks.name: LO/HC Grading Framework (applied to CFO behavior)
frameworks.description: An exercise in applying B165 Learning Outcomes and Habits of Mind to evaluate and critique real-world CFO decision-making patterns from the survey.
frameworks.application: Students graded CFO behaviors using LOs like evaluation/modeling. Ada noted CFOs have sophisticated tools but don't always use the information they produce (e.g., using company-wide discount rates despite knowing about risk-adjusted rates).

frameworks.name: Adjusted Present Value (APV)
frameworks.description: Three-component valuation: (1) Unlevered project value (discount FCF at cost of equity), (2) Present value of tax shields from debt, (3) Less expected costs of financial distress. Provides decomposition of value sources unlike WACC.
frameworks.application: Applied to a perpetuity project: $12M unlevered value + $4M tax shield = $16M total. Used to show how financing decisions affect project value and to determine maximum willingness to pay.

frameworks.name: Incremental Cash Flow Analysis for Replacement Decisions
frameworks.description: Compare two scenarios (replace vs. keep) and calculate only the differences in cash flows at each time period. Include: changes in operating expenses, depreciation differences, tax-adjusted sale of old equipment, and any working capital changes.
frameworks.application: Applied in pre-classwork spreadsheet: old machinery replacement with new MACRS-depreciated equipment, calculating incremental operating cost savings, differential depreciation tax shields, and tax-adjusted disposal value.

frameworks.name: CAPM + Hamada's Equation
frameworks.description: Use Capital Asset Pricing Model to determine required equity return. Hamada's equation converts between unlevered beta (pure business risk) and levered beta (includes financial risk from debt). Levered beta = Unlevered beta x [1 + (1 - tax rate) x (Debt/Equity)].
frameworks.application: Referenced as the method to calculate the increased required return on equity (from 10% to 15% in the example) when a company takes on $10M in debt.

frameworks.name: Perpetuity Valuation
frameworks.description: PV = CF / r for a constant perpetual cash flow. Special case of Gordon Growth Model with g = 0. Applied both to project cash flows and to tax shield valuation.
frameworks.application: Scenario 1: $1.2M / 10% = $12M project value. Tax shield: $300K / 5% = $6M (or $200K / 5% = $4M depending on parameters). Also used for perpetual debt rollover assumption.

frameworks.name: MACRS Depreciation
frameworks.description: Government-prescribed accelerated depreciation schedule. Assets classified into recovery periods (3-year, 5-year, 7-year, etc.) with statutory percentages applied to original cost basis. Sums to 100% over recovery period.
frameworks.application: Used in replacement decision pre-classwork to depreciate new machinery faster than straight-line, generating larger early-year tax shields and improving NPV.

frameworks.name: Replacement Decision NPV/IRR Analysis
frameworks.description: Build two separate cash flow models (status quo vs. new investment), calculate NPV and IRR for each, and compare. Preferred over direct incremental cash flow calculation for transparency, error detection, and stakeholder communication.
frameworks.application: Applied to Aurora Textile's decision to replace old spinning equipment with the Zinsser machine. Two separate spreadsheet models were built and then compared to find incremental value.

frameworks.name: Investment Horizon Sensitivity Framework
frameworks.description: Model project NPV across different assumed company survival horizons (1 year, 2 years, ..., 10 years), incorporating after-tax salvage values at each possible exit point, to assess how survival risk affects investment attractiveness.
frameworks.application: Applied in the second-half breakout activity to show that Aurora's investment NPV becomes severely negative if the company only survives 1-2 years, which is exactly what happened in reality.

frameworks.name: After-Tax Salvage Value Calculation
frameworks.description: After-tax salvage = Sale price + Tax rate * (Book value - Sale price). For zero sale price: After-tax salvage = Tax rate * Book value. For 25% of book value sale: After-tax salvage = 0.25 * BV + 0.36 * 0.75 * BV.
frameworks.application: Used to calculate terminal cash flows at various exit points in the investment horizon sensitivity model.

frameworks.name: DCF/NPV for IT Investment Evaluation
frameworks.description: Standard discounted cash flow analysis applied to IT projects, modeling cost savings, revenue changes, depreciation, and tax effects over the project life
frameworks.application: Applied to two scenarios of a software upgrade: Scenario 1 focused on cost savings (NPV = $4,600), Scenario 2 on price reduction and volume increase (NPV = $3,700); discussed limitations for capturing intangible benefits

frameworks.name: Real Options Valuation
frameworks.description: Treating investment decisions as options that provide future flexibility; includes options to delay, expand, or abandon; helps quantify soft/strategic benefits that NPV misses
frameworks.application: Proposed as a complement to NPV for IT investments to capture strategic flexibility; also applied to the idea of staging/piloting IT investments rather than committing all at once; connected to breaking large projects into smaller sequential investments

frameworks.name: Net Working Capital Analysis
frameworks.description: Analyzing current assets minus current liabilities (AR, AP, inventory, cash) to understand how IT investments affect operational capital requirements
frameworks.application: Discussed how IT tools (CRM, ERP, billing platforms) can improve each component: faster AR collection, automated AP for early payment discounts, better inventory forecasting, and reduced idle cash

frameworks.name: IT Investment Governance/Approval Framework
frameworks.description: A tiered approval process for IT expenditures with dollar thresholds ($5K, $10K, $50K), business case justification requirements, CIO/CFO approval chains, and post-implementation audits
frameworks.application: Evaluated through role-play exercise; students identified weaknesses including missing risk assessment, potential for gaming, bureaucratic drag on innovation, power dynamics, and need for cross-departmental alignment

frameworks.name: Sensitivity Analysis
frameworks.description: Testing how changes in key assumptions affect project outcomes, particularly when assumptions are uncertain
frameworks.application: Recommended for the IT investment discount rate (WACC) since project-specific risk is hard to determine; also implicitly applied to cost reduction assumptions and the $27,000 additional expense figure

frameworks.name: Operating Leverage Analysis
frameworks.description: Framework for evaluating the ratio of fixed to variable costs and its impact on profit sensitivity. Measured as contribution margin / EBIT. Analogous to financial leverage but applied to operating cost structure rather than capital structure.
frameworks.application: Applied to the Fonderia di Torino case to evaluate whether replacing variable labor costs with a fixed-cost machine is value-enhancing, considering volume expectations and risk.

frameworks.name: Leverage as Volatility Amplifier
frameworks.description: Both financial and operating leverage amplify the volatility of returns to equity holders. When revenue exceeds the fixed cost threshold, returns are magnified upward. When revenue falls below, losses are magnified. The professor explicitly drew the parallel between the two types.
frameworks.application: Used to explain why the Fonderia machine investment and the WeWork real estate strategy both carry risk - fixed cost commitments amplify downside when volume disappoints.

frameworks.name: NPV Analysis for Lease vs. Buy
frameworks.description: Compare the present value of all cash flows under leasing (lease payments, tax effects) against all cash flows under buying (purchase price, maintenance, depreciation tax shields, residual value). Use after-tax cost of debt as the discount rate because both options involve fixed contractual obligations with no operational risk differential.
frameworks.application: Applied to a hypothetical equipment purchase of $200,000 (100% debt financed) vs. leasing at $57,000/year over 4 years. The NPV of owning was approximately -$126,000. Leasing showed a financial advantage on headline numbers.

frameworks.name: IFRS 16 / ASC 842 Lease Accounting Framework
frameworks.description: Post-2019 accounting standards requiring both operating and finance leases to be recognized on the balance sheet as right-of-use assets and lease liabilities. Differences remain on the income statement: operating leases show a single expense in EBIT, while finance leases show depreciation in EBIT and interest expense below EBIT.
frameworks.application: Used to understand why the off-balance-sheet advantage of operating leases has disappeared, and how different lease classifications still affect reported profitability metrics differently.

frameworks.name: Comparative Advantage / Market Efficiency in Leasing
frameworks.description: In an efficient market, any advantage of leasing vs. buying should be priced away. Persistent advantages exist only where there are genuine comparative advantages between lessor and lessee: access to capital markets, tax position differences, economies of scale in maintenance/servicing, manufacturer affiliation, or superior ability to manage asset obsolescence.
frameworks.application: Applied to evaluate whether technology obsolescence truly favors leasing, and whether non-profits can negotiate sharing of tax shield benefits with lessors.

frameworks.name: Financial vs. Operational Hedging Framework
frameworks.description: A framework for evaluating two approaches to currency risk management: financial hedging (using instruments like forwards, futures, options) for short-term, defined exposures versus operational hedging (restructuring operations geographically) for long-term competitive positioning.
frameworks.application: Applied throughout the session to evaluate when each approach is appropriate, considering factors like time horizon, cost, flexibility, expertise required, and strategic fit.

frameworks.name: Operational Hedging Strategies Taxonomy
frameworks.description: A classification of operational hedging approaches including: (1) risk shifting (invoicing in home currency), (2) leading and lagging (timing payments), (3) real options through multiple plant locations, and (4) natural hedging through foreign-currency borrowing.
frameworks.application: Students discussed each strategy with real-world examples and identified conditions under which each is most effective or limited.

frameworks.name: Money Market Hedge
frameworks.description: A hedging technique that uses present value discounting: buy foreign currency today at the spot rate, invest it at the foreign interest rate so it grows to match the future payable amount. The cost is the amount of home currency needed today.
frameworks.application: Applied in pre-class work scenarios; students worked through calculations of present value of yen payable and conversion at spot rate to determine dollar cost.

frameworks.name: Adjusted Present Value (APV)
frameworks.description: Valuation approach that starts with unlevered (all-equity) project value and adds financing side effects: interest tax shields, financial distress costs. In multinational context, extended to consider where debt is located (subsidiary vs. parent), different tax shield amounts, and how local-currency debt reduces FX risk (adjusting the all-equity beta via Hamada's equation)
frameworks.application: Applied to the Brazilian subsidiary investment to compare three financing scenarios: all equity, USD debt, and BRL debt; showed how each produces different risk profiles and tax shield values

frameworks.name: Interest Rate Parity (No-Arbitrage Forward Rates)
frameworks.description: Forward exchange rates should offset interest rate differentials between two currencies so that no risk-free arbitrage exists from borrowing in one currency and investing in another
frameworks.application: Used to critique the purple activity's assumption of BRL appreciation despite Brazil's 14% interest rate; in reality, forward rates would show BRL depreciation

frameworks.name: Hamada's Equation (Extended for FX Risk)
frameworks.description: Equation relating levered and unlevered betas; professor noted it can be extended to account for the volatility reduction when local-currency debt provides a natural hedge, thereby reducing the all-equity discount rate
frameworks.application: Conceptually applied to show that BRL debt should reduce the project's unlevered discount rate from the base 20% because net cash flow volatility is lower

frameworks.name: Sensitivity / Scenario Analysis
frameworks.description: Testing how changes in key assumptions affect NPV; professor emphasized this is more transparent than embedding risk adjustments into discount rates
frameworks.application: Recommended for exchange rate forecasts, salvage value (amount and timing), and overall project viability assessment

frameworks.name: Operational Hedging + Financial Hedging (Combined Approach)
frameworks.description: First minimize FX exposure operationally by matching revenue and cost currencies (including debt); then hedge remaining exposure financially with derivatives. This two-step approach minimizes total hedging cost
frameworks.application: Final synthesis of the session: BRL costs and BRL debt reduce the amount that needs to be converted and financially hedged

frameworks.name: Entropy Index for Diversification
frameworks.description: Sum of (Pi * ln(1/Pi)) across all segments, where Pi is the proportion of total revenue from segment i. Higher values indicate more diversification. Maximum entropy occurs at equal splits.
frameworks.application: Used to compare diversification levels: a 90-10 split scores 0.325 while a 95-2.5-2.5 split scores 0.233, showing that revenue concentration matters more than number of segments.

frameworks.name: Herfindahl Index
frameworks.description: Sum of squared proportions of total revenue by segment. Alternative to entropy for measuring concentration/diversification.
frameworks.application: Mentioned as an academic alternative to entropy; both try to capture the degree of revenue concentration across segments.

frameworks.name: Tobin's Q
frameworks.description: Ratio of market value of the firm (equity + liabilities at market value) to replacement cost of assets. In practice, book value substitutes for replacement cost. Values above 1 suggest value creation; below 1 suggest value destruction.
frameworks.application: Used in the in-class exercise to evaluate whether diversified conglomerates create or destroy value relative to focused industry peers. Companies 3 and 6 had Q < 1 (trouble), while conglomerate had Q = 1.1 (ambiguous without knowing pre-merger baseline).

frameworks.name: Product-Geographic Diversification Matrix (from Kim Hong reading)
frameworks.description: 2x2 classification of companies along two dimensions: related vs. unrelated product diversification, and high vs. low global market diversification. Research finds related-industry + high-global performs best.
frameworks.application: Students classified Tata, GE, Marriott, Toyota, Lego, Carlsberg, and Danaher into the matrix during breakout activity. Most large companies fell into high-global categories since large firms naturally expand globally.

frameworks.name: Cross-sectional Diversification Discount Analysis
frameworks.description: Method of comparing diversified firm value to the weighted average value of comparable focused firms in each business segment. If the diversified firm's Tobin's Q is below the weighted industry average, a diversification discount exists.
frameworks.application: Applied in class exercise: conglomerate Q of 1.1 vs. industry averages of 1.2 and 1.5 suggested a discount, but professor showed this is misleading without knowing starting conditions of merged firms.

frameworks.name: Existing Assets vs. Growth Assets Decomposition
frameworks.description: Total firm value = Value of existing assets (NOPAT/WACC as zero-growth perpetuity) + Value of growth assets (total DCF value minus existing asset value). For mature companies, existing asset value dominates; for growth companies, growth asset value dominates.
frameworks.application: Applied in the pre-classwork model to separate the value of a mature company into its two components, helping identify how much value comes from steady-state operations versus future reinvestment.

frameworks.name: ROIC x Reinvestment Rate Growth Model
frameworks.description: Expected growth rate in NOPAT = Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) x Reinvestment Rate. Assumes stable ROIC and that growth comes only from expanding the asset base. Analogous to sustainable growth rate (ROE x retention ratio) but for total capital.
frameworks.application: Used to forecast the growth rate in the DCF model. In the example, 18% ROIC x ~16% reinvestment rate = ~3% growth rate.

frameworks.name: Net Reinvestment Calculation
frameworks.description: Net Reinvestment = CapEx - Depreciation + Change in Working Capital. Depreciation represents the maintenance component of CapEx; only excess CapEx above depreciation actually increases the asset base.
frameworks.application: Applied in the spreadsheet model (line 15) to determine how much of the company's spending actually grows assets versus merely maintains them.

frameworks.name: DCF Valuation for Mature Companies
frameworks.description: Standard DCF using NOPAT-based free cash flows, with growth rate derived from ROIC x reinvestment rate, discounted at WACC. Terminal value based on perpetuity growth. More reliable for mature firms due to stable cash flows and extensive historical data.
frameworks.application: Full DCF model built in pre-classwork and reviewed in class, projecting cash flows, discounting them, and finding NPV.

frameworks.name: Valuation Multiples Cross-Check
frameworks.description: Using comparable company multiples (P/E, EV/EBITDA, Price/Sales) from the same industry to sanity-check DCF results. Assumes market is approximately correct on average but has limitations including comparability issues, market sentiment bias, and capital structure differences.
frameworks.application: Applied as Scenario 2 in the class activity to verify whether the DCF-derived valuation was reasonable relative to industry peers.

frameworks.name: Capital Structure Sensitivity Analysis
frameworks.description: Modeling the effect of changing debt-to-capital ratio on cost of equity, cost of debt, WACC, and overall firm value. Used to identify potential value creation from recapitalization.
frameworks.application: Company modeled at 20% debt-to-capital and then at 40%. Value increased from 5162 to 5507, demonstrating that the company was underleveraged and could create value by taking on more debt.

frameworks.name: Pecking Order Theory
frameworks.description: Hierarchy of financing preferences: (1) internal funds, (2) debt, (3) equity, driven by information asymmetry and issuance costs
frameworks.application: Applied to explain Microsoft's actual behavior - preferring internal funds even when trade-off theory suggested more debt would be optimal; also demonstrated through the IRR comparison spreadsheet

frameworks.name: Trade-Off Theory
frameworks.description: Optimal capital structure balances tax shield benefits of debt against financial distress costs; predicts target leverage ratio that minimizes WACC
frameworks.application: Used to argue Microsoft had significant untapped value from not borrowing more, especially at near-zero rates in 2021-2022; the 2010 study suggested 37% debt ratio as optimal for Microsoft

frameworks.name: Hamada's Equation (Levering/Unlevering Beta)
frameworks.description: Relates levered beta to unlevered beta: Beta_L = Beta_U * (1 + D/E). Separates business risk (unlevered beta) from financial risk (additional leverage component). Linear in leverage
frameworks.application: Used in the pre-classwork model to compute cost of equity at various debt levels via CAPM; professor demonstrated its limitations by noting it misses non-linear financial distress costs

frameworks.name: CAPM (Capital Asset Pricing Model)
frameworks.description: Cost of equity = risk-free rate + beta * market risk premium; uses levered beta to incorporate financial risk
frameworks.application: Used in the pre-classwork model (row 14) to calculate cost of equity at different leverage levels using the levered betas from Hamada's equation

frameworks.name: APV (Adjusted Present Value)
frameworks.description: Firm value = unlevered firm value + PV(tax shield) - PV(financial distress costs); separates the valuation into component parts
frameworks.application: Referenced as the framework where the 'soft' distress costs discussed (customer loss, employee flight, fire sales, etc.) would be subtracted

frameworks.name: Merton Model / Equity as Call Option
frameworks.description: When debt is high, equity functions like a call option on firm assets with strike price equal to face value of debt; uses Black-Scholes for valuation
frameworks.application: Raised to explain non-linear risk behavior: when debt is very high, managers have incentive for excessive risk-taking since equity holders have limited downside but unlimited upside

frameworks.name: Lifecycle-Based Capital Structure Analysis
frameworks.description: Partitioning firms by lifecycle stage (introduction, growth, maturity, decline) to understand how optimal leverage, speed of adjustment, and financing preferences vary
frameworks.application: Central analytical framework for the session; used to structure the empirical study design and to contextualize Microsoft's decisions as a mature firm

frameworks.name: DuPont Identity (Equity Multiplier)
frameworks.description: ROE = ROA x Equity Multiplier. By dividing ROE by ROA, you can infer the degree of financial leverage without directly observing the balance sheet debt levels.
frameworks.application: Used to compare leverage across Hill Country (~1.25x), Snyder's (~2x), and PepsiCo (~4x) from their published ROE and ROA figures.

frameworks.name: Adjusted Present Value (APV)
frameworks.description: Firm value = Value of unlevered operations + PV of tax shields - PV of financial distress costs. Separates the value effects of financing from operations.
frameworks.application: Professor used APV as the theoretical framework to explain why no single metric (EPS, PV of tax shields) captures the total value created by recapitalization; all three APV components must be estimated.

frameworks.name: Interest Coverage Ratio as Risk Metric
frameworks.description: EBIT / Interest Expense. Higher ratios indicate lower probability of default. Used by rating agencies as a primary input to credit ratings.
frameworks.application: Applied to Hill Country's projected financials at different debt levels to estimate expected credit ratings (AA at 37x coverage, down to B at ~4.5x coverage).

frameworks.name: Positive Leverage Test (ROA vs. Cost of Debt)
frameworks.description: If interest rate on debt < ROA, leverage is positive (value-creating for equity holders). If interest rate > ROA, leverage is negative (value-destroying).
frameworks.application: Arushi applied this to Hill Country: ROA ~10%, so debt is value-creating as long as interest rate stays below 10%.

frameworks.name: Sustainable Growth Rate (SGR)
frameworks.description: Maximum growth rate achievable using only retained earnings, calculated as ROE x retention ratio. Indicates unused growth capacity if actual growth is below SGR.
frameworks.application: Arushi calculated Hill Country's SGR at ~9% vs. actual growth of 6-8%, showing the company already had unused growth capacity that would widen further under recapitalization.

frameworks.name: Share Repurchase Value Transfer Analysis
frameworks.description: When repurchasing shares, the price paid determines whether value is created for or transferred away from remaining shareholders. Overpaying transfers value to exiting shareholders.
frameworks.application: Professor illustrated with a hypothetical ($1M for one share) that repurchase price must be at or below fair value to benefit remaining shareholders.

frameworks.name: Modified CAPM for International Cost of Equity
frameworks.description: Cost of Equity = Risk-free rate + Beta x Mature Market Risk Premium + Country Risk Premium. Alternative: Cost of Equity = Risk-free rate + Beta x (Mature Market Risk Premium + Country Risk Premium). Extended version includes lambda for company-specific country risk exposure.
frameworks.application: Used in Activity 2 to calculate cost of equity for subsidiaries in Brazil, Thailand, and Kenya using Damodaran's data

frameworks.name: Damodaran's Country Risk Premium Calculation
frameworks.description: Country Risk Premium = Default Spread x (Volatility of Equity / Volatility of Debt). Uses emerging market equity ETF / emerging market bond ETF ratio (approximately 1.5x) as the volatility multiplier for all emerging markets due to illiquidity in individual country markets.
frameworks.application: Applied to convert sovereign default spreads into equity country risk premiums for Brazil, Thailand, and Kenya

frameworks.name: Perfect Capital Markets Framework (Modigliani-Miller Context)
frameworks.description: Theoretical baseline assuming no transaction costs, no arbitrage, no information asymmetry, no interest rate spreads, competitive markets, unrestricted capital flows, and efficient pricing. Used as a contrast to identify real-world frictions in international finance.
frameworks.application: Opening discussion established the theoretical baseline before examining how international markets deviate from these assumptions

frameworks.name: Subsidiary Capital Structure Decision Framework
frameworks.description: Four-box analysis of capital structure options for foreign subsidiaries: (1) Same as parent, (2) More leverage than parent, (3) Less leverage than parent, (4) Independent local optimization. Each evaluated based on tax shield benefits, currency hedging, political risk hedging, capital controls, agency costs, and operational complexity.
frameworks.application: Breakout Activity 1 where students filled in pros and cons for each approach in the context of an emerging market subsidiary

frameworks.name: Lambda (Country Risk Exposure) Model
frameworks.description: Extension of the country risk premium model where a company-specific lambda factor scales the country risk premium based on the proportion of revenue from the country and the nature of operations (e.g., software sales vs. physical operations). Lambda of 1 = average exposure; lower lambda for less operationally exposed firms.
frameworks.application: Discussed as refinement to the basic cost of equity calculation; professor noted Damodaran used 0.25 as a baseline example

frameworks.name: Three Theories of Dividend Policy
frameworks.description: Framework classifying dividend policy theories into: (1) Dividend Irrelevance (Modigliani-Miller, perfect markets assumption), (2) Clientele Theory (different investor groups prefer different policies based on taxes, income needs, risk), (3) Information Asymmetry/Signaling (dividends convey credible information about firm quality)
